**Malaysia**[[d]](#cite_note-22) is a country in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"). A [federal](/wiki/Federation "Federation") [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), it consists of [13 states and three federal territories](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia"), separated by the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea") into two regions: [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia") on the [Indochinese Peninsula](/wiki/Indochinese_Peninsula "Indochinese Peninsula") and [East Malaysia](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia") on the island of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"). Peninsular Malaysia shares land and [maritime borders](/wiki/Maritime_boundary "Maritime boundary") with [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), as well as maritime borders with [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam"), and [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"); East Malaysia shares land borders with [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") and Indonesia, and maritime borders with the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") and Vietnam. [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur") is the country's national capital, [largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_and_towns_in_Malaysia_by_population "List of cities and towns in Malaysia by population"), and the seat of the [legislative branch](/wiki/Parliament_of_Malaysia "Parliament of Malaysia") of the [federal government](/wiki/Government_of_Malaysia "Government of Malaysia"), while [Putrajaya](/wiki/Putrajaya "Putrajaya") is the federal administrative capital, representing the seat of both the [executive branch](/wiki/Government_of_Malaysia#Executive "Government of Malaysia") and the [judicial branch](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Malaysia "Judiciary of Malaysia") of the federal government. With a population of over 34 million, it is the world's [42nd-most populous country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population "List of countries by population").

The country has [its origins](/wiki/History_of_Malaysia "History of Malaysia") in the [Malay kingdoms](/wiki/Malay_Kingdom_(disambiguation) "Malay Kingdom (disambiguation)"), which, from the 18th century on, became subject to the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire"), along with the British [Straits Settlements](/wiki/Straits_Settlements "Straits Settlements") protectorate. During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), [British Malaya](/wiki/British_Malaya "British Malaya"), along with other nearby British and American colonies, was occupied by the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan"). Following three years of occupation, Malaya was briefly unified as the [Malayan Union](/wiki/Malayan_Union "Malayan Union") in 1946 until 1948 when it was restructured as the [Federation of Malaya](/wiki/Federation_of_Malaya "Federation of Malaya"). The country achieved independence on [31 August 1957](/wiki/Independence_Day_(Malaysia) "Independence Day (Malaysia)"). On [16 September 1963](/wiki/Malaysia_Day "Malaysia Day"), independent Malaya united with the then [British crown colonies](/wiki/Crown_colony "Crown colony") of [North Borneo](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_North_Borneo "Crown Colony of North Borneo"), [Sarawak](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Sarawak "Crown Colony of Sarawak"), and [Singapore](/wiki/Colony_of_Singapore "Colony of Singapore") to become Malaysia. In August 1965, Singapore was [separated](/wiki/Singapore_in_Malaysia "Singapore in Malaysia") from the federation and became an [independent country](/wiki/Proclamation_of_Singapore "Proclamation of Singapore").

Malaysia is [tropical](/wiki/Tropical "Tropical") and is one of 17 [megadiverse countries](/wiki/Megadiverse_countries "Megadiverse countries"); it is home to numerous [endemic species](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Malaysia "Wildlife of Malaysia"). The country is [multiethnic](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Malaysia "Ethnic groups in Malaysia") and [multicultural](/wiki/Culture_of_Malaysia "Culture of Malaysia"), which has a significant effect on its politics. About half the population is ethnically [Malay](/wiki/Malaysian_Malays "Malaysian Malays"), with minorities of [Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese"), [Indians](/wiki/Malaysian_Indian "Malaysian Indian"), and [indigenous peoples](/wiki/Orang_Asal "Orang Asal"). The official language is [Malaysian Malay](/wiki/Malaysian_Malay "Malaysian Malay"), a standard form of the [Malay language](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"). [English](/wiki/Malaysian_English "Malaysian English") remains an active second language. While recognising [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") as the [official religion](/wiki/Official_religion "Official religion"), the constitution grants [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion "Freedom of religion") to non-Muslims. The government is modelled on the [Westminster parliamentary system](/wiki/Westminster_parliamentary_system "Westminster parliamentary system"), and the legal system is based on [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law"). The [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") is an [elected monarch](/wiki/Elected_monarch "Elected monarch"), chosen among the [nine state rulers](/wiki/Monarchies_of_Malaysia "Monarchies of Malaysia") every five years. The [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government") is the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia").

[The country's economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Malaysia "Economy of Malaysia") was once heavily reliant on agriculture and primary commodities but is now driven by the services and manufacturing sectors. Malaysia ranks highly in [peacefulness](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index") and [passport strength](/wiki/Henley_Passport_Index "Henley Passport Index") and has a [newly industrialised economy](/wiki/Newly_industrialised_economy "Newly industrialised economy") that is relatively open and state-oriented. The country is a founding member of the [Organisation of Islamic Cooperation](/wiki/Organisation_of_Islamic_Cooperation "Organisation of Islamic Cooperation") (OIC), the [East Asia Summit](/wiki/East_Asia_Summit "East Asia Summit") (EAS), and the [Association of Southeast Asian Nations](/wiki/Association_of_Southeast_Asian_Nations "Association of Southeast Asian Nations") (ASEAN), as well as a member of the [Non-Aligned Movement](/wiki/Non-Aligned_Movement "Non-Aligned Movement") (NAM), the [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations"), and the [Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation](/wiki/Asia-Pacific_Economic_Cooperation "Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation") (APEC).

## Etymology

The name [*Malaysia*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Malaysia "wikt:Malaysia") is a combination of the word [*Malays*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Malays "wikt:Malays") and the Latin-Greek suffix [*-ia*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-ia "wikt:-ia")/[-ία](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/-%CE%AF%CE%B1 "wikt:-ία"),[[17]](#cite_note-:3-23) which can be translated as 'land of the Malays'.[[18]](#cite_note-CIA_Fact_Book-24) Similar-sounding variants have also appeared in accounts older than the 11th century, as [toponyms](/wiki/Toponym "Toponym") for areas in Sumatra or referring to a larger region around the Strait of Malacca.[[19]](#cite_note-Barnard-25) The Sanskrit text *[Vayu Purana](/wiki/Vayu_Purana "Vayu Purana")*, thought to have been in existence since the first millennium CE, mentioned a land named 'Malayadvipa', which was identified by certain scholars as the modern [Malay Peninsula](/wiki/Malay_Peninsula "Malay Peninsula").[[20]](#cite_note-26)[[21]](#cite_note-27)[[22]](#cite_note-28)[[23]](#cite_note-29)[[24]](#cite_note-30) Other notable accounts are by Ptolemy's 2nd-century *[Geographia](/wiki/Geographia "Geographia")*, which used the name *Malayu Kulon* for the west coast of [Golden Chersonese](/wiki/Golden_Chersonese "Golden Chersonese"), and the 7th-century [Yijing](/wiki/Yijing_(monk) "Yijing (monk)")'s account of *Malayu*.[[19]](#cite_note-Barnard-25)

At some point, the [Melayu Kingdom](/wiki/Melayu_Kingdom "Melayu Kingdom") took its name from the *Sungai Melayu*.[[25]](#cite_note-SejarahBahasaMelayu-31)[[26]](#cite_note-32) *Melayu* then became associated with [Srivijaya](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya"),[[19]](#cite_note-Barnard-25) and remained associated with various parts of Sumatra, especially Palembang, where the founder of the [Malacca Sultanate](/wiki/Malacca_Sultanate "Malacca Sultanate") is thought to have come from.[[27]](#cite_note-Andaya-33) It is only thought to have developed into an [ethnonym](/wiki/Ethnonym "Ethnonym") as Malacca became a regional power in the 15th century. [Islamisation](/wiki/Islamisation "Islamisation") established an [ethnoreligious](/wiki/Ethnoreligious "Ethnoreligious") identity in Malacca, with the term *Melayu* beginning to appear as interchangeable with *Melakans*. It may have specifically referred to local Malay speakers who were loyal to the Malaccan Sultan. The initial Portuguese use of *Malayos* reflected this, referring only to the ruling people of Malacca. The prominence of traders from Malacca led *Melayu* to be associated with Muslim traders, and from there became associated with the wider cultural and linguistic group.[[19]](#cite_note-Barnard-25) Malacca and later [Johor](/wiki/Johor_Sultanate "Johor Sultanate") claimed they were the centre of Malay culture, a position supported by the British which led to the term *Malay* becoming more usually linked to the Malay peninsula rather than Sumatra.[[27]](#cite_note-Andaya-33)

Before the onset of European colonisation, the Malay Peninsula was known natively as *[Tanah Melayu](/wiki/Tanah_Melayu "Tanah Melayu")* ('Malay Land').[[28]](#cite_note-34) Under a racial classification created by German scholar [Johann Friedrich Blumenbach](/wiki/Johann_Friedrich_Blumenbach "Johann Friedrich Blumenbach"), the natives of [maritime Southeast Asia](/wiki/Maritime_Southeast_Asia "Maritime Southeast Asia") were grouped into a single category, the [Malay race](/wiki/Malay_race "Malay race").[[29]](#cite_note-35)[[30]](#cite_note-36) Following the expedition of French navigator [Jules Dumont d'Urville](/wiki/Jules_Dumont_d%27Urville "Jules Dumont d'Urville") to [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania") in 1826, he later proposed the terms of *Malaysia*, *[Micronesia](/wiki/Micronesia "Micronesia")*, and *[Melanesia](/wiki/Melanesia "Melanesia")* to the *[Société de Géographie](/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_de_G%C3%A9ographie "Société de Géographie")* in 1831, distinguishing these Pacific cultures and island groups from the existing term *[Polynesia](/wiki/Polynesia "Polynesia")*. Dumont d'Urville described Malaysia as "an area commonly known as the East Indies".[[31]](#cite_note-37)

In 1850, the English ethnologist [George Samuel Windsor Earl](/wiki/George_Samuel_Windsor_Earl "George Samuel Windsor Earl"), writing in the *Journal of the Indian Archipelago and Eastern Asia*, proposed naming the islands of Southeast Asia as "Melayunesia" or "Indunesia", favouring the former.[[32]](#cite_note-38) The name Malaysia gained some use to label what is now the Malay Archipelago.[[33]](#cite_note-39) In modern terminology, *Malay* remains the name of an [ethnoreligious group](/wiki/Ethnoreligious_group "Ethnoreligious group") of [Austronesian people](/wiki/Austronesian_people "Austronesian people") predominantly inhabiting the Malay Peninsula and portions of the adjacent islands of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), including the east coast of Sumatra, the coast of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), and smaller islands that lie between these areas.[[34]](#cite_note-40)

The state that gained independence from the United Kingdom in 1957 took the name *the [Federation of Malaya](/wiki/Federation_of_Malaya "Federation of Malaya")*, chosen in preference to other potential names such as *Malaysia*[[35]](#cite_note-MALAYA'S_FUTURE_NAME-41) and *[Langkasuka](/wiki/Langkasuka "Langkasuka")*,[[35]](#cite_note-MALAYA'S_FUTURE_NAME-41) after the historic kingdom located at the upper section of the Malay Peninsula in the first millennium CE.[[36]](#cite_note-Mapping-42)[[37]](#cite_note-43)

In 1963, the name *Malaysia* was adopted when the existing states of the Federation of Malaya, plus Singapore, North Borneo, and Sarawak, formed a new federation.[[38]](#cite_note-Bound_for_Glory-44)[[e]](#cite_note-46) One theory posits the name was chosen so that *si* represented the inclusion of Singapore, North Borneo, and Sarawak.[[38]](#cite_note-Bound_for_Glory-44) Politicians in the Philippines contemplated renaming their state Malaysia before the modern country took the name.[[40]](#cite_note-47)

## History

Main article: [History of Malaysia](/wiki/History_of_Malaysia "History of Malaysia")

[![Map showing the extent of the Malacca Sultanate, covering much of the Malay Peninsula and some of Sumatra](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/Malacca_Sultanate_en.svg/250px-Malacca_Sultanate_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Malacca_Sultanate_en.svg)

The [Malacca Sultanate](/wiki/Malacca_Sultanate "Malacca Sultanate") played a major role in spreading Islam throughout the Malay Archipelago.

Evidence of modern human habitation in Malaysia dates back 40,000 years.[[41]](#cite_note-48) In the Malay Peninsula, the first inhabitants are thought to be [Negritos](/wiki/Negritos "Negritos").[[42]](#cite_note-49)[[43]](#cite_note-50) Areas of Malaysia participated in the Maritime Jade Road between 2000 BC to 1000 AD.[[44]](#cite_note-51)[[45]](#cite_note-52)[[46]](#cite_note-53)[[47]](#cite_note-54) Traders and settlers from [India](/wiki/India "India") and [China](/wiki/China "China") arrived as early as the first century AD, establishing trading ports and coastal towns in the second and third centuries. Their presence resulted in strong [Indian](/wiki/India "India") and [Chinese](/wiki/China "China") influences on the local cultures, and the people of the Malay Peninsula adopted the religions of [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"). [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") inscriptions appear as early as the fourth or fifth century.[[48]](#cite_note-55) The Kingdom of [Langkasuka](/wiki/Langkasuka "Langkasuka") arose around the second century in the northern area of the Malay Peninsula, lasting until about the 15th century.[[36]](#cite_note-Mapping-42) Between the 7th and 13th centuries, much of the southern Malay Peninsula was part of the maritime [Srivijayan](/wiki/Srivijaya "Srivijaya") empire. By the 13th and the 14th century, the [Majapahit](/wiki/Majapahit "Majapahit") empire had successfully wrested control over most of the peninsula and the Malay Archipelago from Srivijaya.[[49]](#cite_note-56) In the early 15th century, [Parameswara](/wiki/Parameswara_(king) "Parameswara (king)"), a runaway king of the former [Kingdom of Singapura](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Singapura "Kingdom of Singapura") linked to the old Srivijayan court, founded the [Malacca Sultanate](/wiki/Malacca_Sultanate "Malacca Sultanate").[[50]](#cite_note-57) The spread of Islam increased following Parameswara's conversion to that religion. Malacca was an important commercial centre during this time, attracting trade from around the region.[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58)

[![Dutch fleet vs Portuguese armada](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/AMH-6472-KB_Battle_for_Malacca_between_the_VOC_fleet_and_the_Portuguese%2C_1606.jpg/250px-AMH-6472-KB_Battle_for_Malacca_between_the_VOC_fleet_and_the_Portuguese%2C_1606.jpg)](/wiki/File:AMH-6472-KB_Battle_for_Malacca_between_the_VOC_fleet_and_the_Portuguese,_1606.jpg)

The Dutch fleet battling with the Portuguese armada as part of the [Dutch–Portuguese War](/wiki/Dutch%E2%80%93Portuguese_War "Dutch–Portuguese War") in 1606 to gain control of [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca_City "Malacca City")

In 1511, [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca "Malacca") was conquered by [Portugal](/wiki/Portuguese_Malacca "Portuguese Malacca"),[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) after which it was taken by the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Malacca "Dutch Malacca") in 1641. In 1786, the [British Empire](/wiki/British_Malaya "British Malaya") established a presence in Malaya, when the Sultan of Kedah leased [Penang Island](/wiki/Penang_Island "Penang Island") to the British [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company"). The British obtained the town of Singapore in 1819,[[52]](#cite_note-59) and in 1824 took control of Malacca following the [Anglo-Dutch Treaty](/wiki/Anglo-Dutch_Treaty_of_1824 "Anglo-Dutch Treaty of 1824"). By 1826, the British directly controlled Penang, Malacca, Singapore, and the island of [Labuan](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Labuan "Crown Colony of Labuan"), which they established as the crown colony of the [Straits Settlements](/wiki/Straits_Settlements "Straits Settlements"). By the 20th century, the states of [Pahang](/wiki/Pahang "Pahang"), [Selangor](/wiki/Selangor "Selangor"), [Perak](/wiki/Perak "Perak"), and [Negeri Sembilan](/wiki/Negeri_Sembilan "Negeri Sembilan"), known together as the [Federated Malay States](/wiki/Federated_Malay_States "Federated Malay States"), had British [residents](/wiki/Resident_(title) "Resident (title)") appointed to advise the Malay rulers, to whom the rulers were bound to defer by treaty.[[53]](#cite_note-60) The remaining five states on the peninsula, known as the [Unfederated Malay States](/wiki/Unfederated_Malay_States "Unfederated Malay States"), while not directly under British rule, also accepted British advisers around the turn of the 20th century. Development on the peninsula and Borneo were generally separate until the 19th century. Under British rule the [immigration](/wiki/Immigration "Immigration") of Chinese and Indians to serve as labourers was encouraged.[[54]](#cite_note-61) The area that is now [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") came under British control as [North Borneo](/wiki/North_Borneo "North Borneo") when both the [Sultan of Brunei](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Brunei "Sultanate of Brunei") and the [Sultan of Sulu](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Sulu "Sultanate of Sulu") transferred their respective territorial rights of ownership, between 1877 and 1878.[[55]](#cite_note-62) In 1842, Sarawak was ceded by the Sultan of Brunei to [James Brooke](/wiki/James_Brooke "James Brooke"), whose successors ruled as the [White Rajahs](/wiki/White_Rajahs "White Rajahs") over an independent [kingdom](/wiki/Raj_of_Sarawak "Raj of Sarawak") until 1946, when it became a [crown colony](/wiki/Crown_Colony_of_Sarawak "Crown Colony of Sarawak").[[56]](#cite_note-63)

The [Klang War](/wiki/Klang_War "Klang War") (1867–1874) began as a dispute between Raja Abdullah and Raja Mahadi over the Klang chieftaincy, with Mahadi rejecting Abdullah's appointment by Sultan [Abdul Samad of Selangor](/wiki/Abdul_Samad_of_Selangor "Abdul Samad of Selangor"). The conflict drew in rival Chinese [secret societies](/wiki/Secret_societies "Secret societies")—the [Ghee Hin Kongsi](/wiki/Ghee_Hin_Kongsi "Ghee Hin Kongsi") largely backing Mahadi and the [Hai San Secret Society](/wiki/Hai_San_Secret_Society "Hai San Secret Society") supporting Abdullah—as well as Bugis-descended and Malay chiefs who viewed Mahadi as more legitimate. Fighting spread after Mahadi captured the Klang Fort and Abdullah fled to Malacca, disrupting tin production, trade routes, and British commercial interests in the Klang Valley. At the same time, the neighbouring [Larut Wars](/wiki/Larut_Wars "Larut Wars") (1861–1874) between the Hai San and Ghee Hin in Perak similarly destabilised tin-rich districts and alarmed British merchants. Appeals from Malay chiefs for mediation combined with British concerns over regional disorder paved the way for formal intervention, culminating in the [Pangkor Treaty](/wiki/Pangkor_Treaty "Pangkor Treaty") of 1874 which established a system of British residents in the Malay states.[[57]](#cite_note-Azmi2012-64) As [Frank Swettenham](/wiki/Frank_Swettenham "Frank Swettenham") later wrote, "Though the circumstances demanded intervention, we came in to the Malay States at the invitation of the Rulers to teach them a better form of government."[[58]](#cite_note-Swettenham1903-65)[[59]](#cite_note-AzmiPerak-66)[[60]](#cite_note-LarutCommission-67)

In the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), the [Japanese Army](/wiki/Imperial_Japanese_Army "Imperial Japanese Army") [invaded](/wiki/Japanese_invasion_of_Malaya "Japanese invasion of Malaya") and occupied [Malaya](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_Malaya "Japanese occupation of Malaya"), [North Borneo, Sarawak](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_British_Borneo "Japanese occupation of British Borneo"), and [Singapore](/wiki/Japanese_occupation_of_Singapore "Japanese occupation of Singapore") for over three years.[[61]](#cite_note-68) During this time, ethnic tensions were raised and nationalism grew.[[62]](#cite_note-69) Popular support for independence increased after Malaya was reconquered by Allied forces.[[63]](#cite_note-70) Post-war British plans to unite the administration of Malaya under a single crown colony called the [Malayan Union](/wiki/Malayan_Union "Malayan Union") met with strong opposition from the [Malays](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)"), who opposed the weakening of the Malay rulers and the granting of citizenship to the [ethnic Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese"). The Malayan Union, established in 1946, and consisting of all the [British possessions](/wiki/British_possessions "British possessions") in the Malay Peninsula except for [Singapore](/wiki/Colony_of_Singapore "Colony of Singapore"), was quickly dissolved and replaced on 1 February 1948 by the [Federation of Malaya](/wiki/Federation_of_Malaya "Federation of Malaya"), which restored the autonomy of the rulers of the Malay states under British protection.[[64]](#cite_note-71)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Police_in_Malayan_Emergency.jpg/250px-Police_in_Malayan_Emergency.jpg)](/wiki/File:Police_in_Malayan_Emergency.jpg)

[Malayan](/wiki/Federation_of_Malaya "Federation of Malaya") police officers questioning an [ethnic Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Malaysian "Chinese Malaysian") civilian during the [Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency") in 1949

During this time, the mostly ethnically Chinese rebels under the leadership of the [Malayan Communist Party](/wiki/Malayan_Communist_Party "Malayan Communist Party") launched guerrilla operations designed to force the British out of Malaya. The [Malayan Emergency (1948–1960)](/wiki/Malayan_Emergency "Malayan Emergency") involved a long anti-insurgency campaign by [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") troops in Malaya.[[65]](#cite_note-72) On 31 August 1957, [Malaya became an independent member](/wiki/Malayan_Declaration_of_Independence "Malayan Declaration of Independence") of the Commonwealth of Nations.[[66]](#cite_note-73) Subsequently, a comprehensive plan was devised to unite Malaya with the crown colonies of North Borneo (known as [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") upon joining), [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"), and [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"). The envisioned federation was originally intended to take place on 31 August 1963, to coincide with the commemoration of Malayan independence. However, due to the necessity of conducting a survey on the level of support for the federation in Sabah and Sarawak by the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), as requested by opponents of the federation such as [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")'s [Sukarno](/wiki/Sukarno "Sukarno") and the [Sarawak United Peoples' Party](/wiki/Sarawak_United_Peoples%27_Party "Sarawak United Peoples' Party"), the date of the federation was postponed until 16 September 1963.[[67]](#cite_note-74)[[68]](#cite_note-75)

The federation brought heightened tensions including a [conflict with Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia%E2%80%93Malaysia_confrontation "Indonesia–Malaysia confrontation") as well as continual conflicts against the Communists in Borneo and the Malay Peninsula, which escalated to the [Sarawak Communist Insurgency](/wiki/Sarawak_Communist_Insurgency "Sarawak Communist Insurgency") and [Second Malayan Emergency](/wiki/Second_Malayan_Emergency "Second Malayan Emergency") together with several other issues such as the [cross-border attacks into Sabah](/wiki/Cross_border_attacks_in_Sabah "Cross border attacks in Sabah") by [Moro pirates](/wiki/Moro_pirates "Moro pirates") from the southern islands of the Philippines, Singapore [being separated from the Federation](/wiki/State_of_Singapore_(Malaysia)#Separation "State of Singapore (Malaysia)") in 1965,[[69]](#cite_note-76)[[70]](#cite_note-77)[[71]](#cite_note-TIME_Art-78) and racial strife. This strife culminated in the [13 May race riots](/wiki/13_May_Incident "13 May Incident") in 1969.[[72]](#cite_note-Race_war-79) After the riots, the controversial [New Economic Policy](/wiki/Malaysian_New_Economic_Policy "Malaysian New Economic Policy") was launched by Prime Minister [Tun Abdul Razak](/wiki/Tun_Abdul_Razak "Tun Abdul Razak"), trying to increase the share of the economy held by the *[bumiputera](/wiki/Bumiputera_(Malaysia) "Bumiputera (Malaysia)")*.[[73]](#cite_note-New_Economic_Policy-80) Under Prime Minister [Mahathir Mohamad](/wiki/Mahathir_Mohamad "Mahathir Mohamad") there was a period of rapid economic growth and urbanization beginning in the 1980s. The economy shifted from being agriculturally based to one based on manufacturing and industry. Numerous mega-projects were completed, such as the [Petronas Towers](/wiki/Petronas_Towers "Petronas Towers"), the [North–South Expressway](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_(Malaysia) "North–South Expressway (Malaysia)"), the [Multimedia Super Corridor](/wiki/Multimedia_Super_Corridor "Multimedia Super Corridor"), and the new federal administrative capital of [Putrajaya](/wiki/Putrajaya "Putrajaya").[[38]](#cite_note-Bound_for_Glory-44)[[74]](#cite_note-81)

In the late 1990s, the [Asian financial crisis](/wiki/Asian_financial_crisis "Asian financial crisis") impacted the country, nearly causing their currency, stock, and property markets to crash; however, they later recovered.[[75]](#cite_note-82) The [1MDB scandal](/wiki/1MDB_scandal "1MDB scandal") was a major global [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Malaysia "Corruption in Malaysia") scandal that implicated then-Prime Minister [Najib Razak](/wiki/Najib_Razak "Najib Razak") in 2015.[[76]](#cite_note-83) The scandal contributed to the first change in the ruling political party since independence in the [2018 general election](/wiki/2018_Malaysian_general_election "2018 Malaysian general election").[[77]](#cite_note-Opposition_scores-84) In the 2020s, the country was gripped by [a political crisis](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Malaysian_political_crisis "2020–21 Malaysian political crisis") that coincided with health and [economic](/wiki/Economic_impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_in_Malaysia "Economic impact of the COVID-19 pandemic in Malaysia") crises caused by the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Malaysia "COVID-19 pandemic in Malaysia").[[78]](#cite_note-85) This was then followed by an earlier [general election](/wiki/2022_Malaysian_general_election "2022 Malaysian general election") in November 2022, which resulted in the first hung parliament in the nation's history.[[79]](#cite_note-86) On 24 November 2022, [Anwar Ibrahim](/wiki/Anwar_Ibrahim "Anwar Ibrahim") was sworn in as the 10th [Prime Minister of Malaysia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia"), leading a [grand coalition](/wiki/Grand_coalition#Malaysia "Grand coalition") government.[[80]](#cite_note-87)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Politics of Malaysia](/wiki/Politics_of_Malaysia "Politics of Malaysia") and [Government of Malaysia](/wiki/Government_of_Malaysia "Government of Malaysia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/Istana_Negara_tepi.jpg/250px-Istana_Negara_tepi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Istana_Negara_tepi.jpg)

[Istana Negara](/wiki/Istana_Negara,_Jalan_Tuanku_Abdul_Halim "Istana Negara, Jalan Tuanku Abdul Halim"), the official residence and office of the [King of Malaysia](/wiki/King_of_Malaysia "King of Malaysia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Perdana_Putra.jpg/250px-Perdana_Putra.jpg)](/wiki/File:Perdana_Putra.jpg)

[Perdana Putra](/wiki/Perdana_Putra "Perdana Putra"), the office of the [Prime Minister of Malaysia](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia")

Malaysia is a [federal](/wiki/Federal_monarchy "Federal monarchy") [constitutional](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") [elective monarchy](/wiki/Elective_monarchy "Elective monarchy") and the only federal country in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia").[[81]](#cite_note-88) The system of government is closely modelled on the [Westminster](/wiki/Westminster_system "Westminster system") parliamentary system, a legacy of [British rule](/wiki/British_Empire "British Empire").[[82]](#cite_note-FITA-89) The head of state is the King, whose official title is the [Yang di-Pertuan Agong](/wiki/Yang_di-Pertuan_Agong "Yang di-Pertuan Agong"). The King is elected to a five-year term by and from among the nine hereditary [rulers](/wiki/Monarchies_of_Malaysia "Monarchies of Malaysia") of the [Malay states](/wiki/Malay_states "Malay states"). The other four states, which have titular [Governors](/wiki/Yang_di-Pertua_Negeri "Yang di-Pertua Negeri"), do not participate in the selection. By informal agreement the position is rotated among the nine,[[82]](#cite_note-FITA-89) and has been held by [Ibrahim Iskandar of Johor](/wiki/Ibrahim_Iskandar_of_Johor "Ibrahim Iskandar of Johor") since 31 January 2024. The King's role has been largely ceremonial since changes to the [constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Malaysia "Constitution of Malaysia") in [1994](/wiki/1993_amendments_to_the_Constitution_of_Malaysia "1993 amendments to the Constitution of Malaysia"), picking ministers and members of the upper house.[[83]](#cite_note-dfat-90)

Legislative power is divided between federal and state legislatures. The bicameral federal [parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Malaysia "Parliament of Malaysia") consists of the [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house"), the [House of Representatives](/wiki/House_of_Representatives_of_Malaysia "House of Representatives of Malaysia") and the [upper house](/wiki/Upper_house "Upper house"), the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Malaysia "Senate of Malaysia").[[84]](#cite_note-91) The 222-member House of Representatives is elected for a maximum term of five years from single-member constituencies. All 70 senators sit for three-year terms; 26 are elected by the 13 state assemblies, and the remaining 44 are appointed by the King upon the Prime Minister's recommendation.[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) The parliament follows a multi-party system and the government is elected through a [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post "First-past-the-post") system.[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58)[[85]](#cite_note-92) Parliamentary [elections](/wiki/Elections_in_Malaysia "Elections in Malaysia") are held at least once every five years.[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) Before 2018, only registered voters aged 21 and above could vote for the members of the House of Representatives and, in most of the states, for the state legislative chamber. Voting is not mandatory.[[86]](#cite_note-93) In July 2019, a bill to lower the [voting age](/wiki/Voting_age "Voting age") to 18 years old was officially passed.[[87]](#cite_note-94)

[![Large building with a series of flags in front of it](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Perdana_Putra_building_2005.jpg/250px-Perdana_Putra_building_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Perdana_Putra_building_2005.jpg)

The [Perdana Putra](/wiki/Perdana_Putra "Perdana Putra") houses the office of Malaysia's [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia").

[Executive power](/wiki/Executive_power "Executive power") is vested in the [Cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Malaysia "Cabinet of Malaysia"), led by the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia"). The prime minister must be a member of the House of Representatives, who in the opinion of His Majesty the King, commands the support of a majority of members. The Cabinet is chosen from members of both houses of Parliament.[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) The Prime Minister is both the [head of cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Malaysia "Cabinet of Malaysia") and the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government").[[83]](#cite_note-dfat-90) As a result of the [2018 general election](/wiki/2018_Malaysian_general_election "2018 Malaysian general election") Malaysia was governed by the [Pakatan Harapan](/wiki/Pakatan_Harapan "Pakatan Harapan") (PH) [political alliance](/wiki/Political_alliance "Political alliance"),[[77]](#cite_note-Opposition_scores-84) although Prime Minister [Mahathir Mohamad](/wiki/Mahathir_Mohamad "Mahathir Mohamad") resigned amid a [political crisis](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Malaysian_political_crisis "2020–21 Malaysian political crisis") in 2020. In March 2020, the [Perikatan Nasional](/wiki/Perikatan_Nasional "Perikatan Nasional") (PN) coalition formed under Prime Minister [Muhyiddin Yassin](/wiki/Muhyiddin_Yassin "Muhyiddin Yassin"),[[88]](#cite_note-95) before Muhyiddin lost majority support and was replaced by deputy Prime Minister [Ismail Sabri Yaakob](/wiki/Ismail_Sabri_Yaakob "Ismail Sabri Yaakob"), a veteran politician from the [United Malays National Organisation](/wiki/United_Malays_National_Organisation "United Malays National Organisation") (UMNO), in August 2021.[[89]](#cite_note-96)[[90]](#cite_note-97) As a result of the [2022 Malaysian general election](/wiki/2022_Malaysian_general_election "2022 Malaysian general election"), a hung parliament was elected. [Anwar Ibrahim](/wiki/Anwar_Ibrahim "Anwar Ibrahim") of the PH coalition was appointed as the new Prime Minister to lead the coalition government of PH, [Barisan Nasional](/wiki/Barisan_Nasional "Barisan Nasional"), [Gabungan Parti Sarawak](/wiki/Gabungan_Parti_Sarawak "Gabungan Parti Sarawak"), [Gabungan Rakyat Sabah](/wiki/Gabungan_Rakyat_Sabah "Gabungan Rakyat Sabah") and several other political parties and independents. Meanwhile, PN, the only political coalition not in the coalition government became the Opposition.

[Malaysia's legal system](/wiki/Law_of_Malaysia "Law of Malaysia") is based on [common law](/wiki/English_law "English law").[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) Although [the judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Malaysia "Judiciary of Malaysia") is theoretically independent, its independence has been called into question and the appointment of judges lacks accountability and transparency.[[91]](#cite_note-98) The highest court in the judicial system is the [Federal Court](/wiki/Federal_Court_of_Malaysia "Federal Court of Malaysia"), followed by the [Court of Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_of_Malaysia "Court of Appeal of Malaysia") and two [high courts](/wiki/High_Courts_of_Malaysia "High Courts of Malaysia"), one for Peninsular Malaysia and one for East Malaysia. Malaysia also has a special court to hear cases brought by or against royalty.[[92]](#cite_note-criminal_court_system-99)

[Race](/wiki/Demographics_of_Malaysia "Demographics of Malaysia") is a significant force in politics.[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) [Affirmative actions](/wiki/Affirmative_action#Malaysia "Affirmative action") such as the [New Economic Policy](/wiki/Malaysian_New_Economic_Policy "Malaysian New Economic Policy")[[73]](#cite_note-New_Economic_Policy-80) and the [National Development Policy](/wiki/National_Development_Policy "National Development Policy") which superseded it, were implemented to advance the standing of the *bumiputera*, consisting of Malays and the indigenous tribes who are considered the original inhabitants of Malaysia, over non-*bumiputera* such as [Malaysian Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese") and [Malaysian Indians](/wiki/Malaysian_Indians "Malaysian Indians").[[93]](#cite_note-100) These policies provide preferential treatment to *bumiputera* in employment, education, scholarships, business, and access to cheaper housing and assisted savings. However, it has generated greater interethnic resentment.[[94]](#cite_note-101) There is ongoing [debate](/wiki/Religion_in_Malaysia#Secularism "Religion in Malaysia") over whether the laws and society of Malaysia should reflect [Islamism](/wiki/Islamism "Islamism") or [secularism](/wiki/Secularism "Secularism").[[95]](#cite_note-102) Islamic criminal laws passed by the [Pan-Malaysian Islamic Party](/wiki/Pan-Malaysian_Islamic_Party "Pan-Malaysian Islamic Party") with the support of UMNO state assemblymen in the state legislative assembly of Kelantan have been unenforced by the federal government on the basis that criminal laws are the responsibility of the federal government.[[96]](#cite_note-103)[[97]](#cite_note-104)[[98]](#cite_note-105)

After UMNO lost power at the [2018 Malaysian general election](/wiki/2018_Malaysian_general_election "2018 Malaysian general election"), Malaysia's ranking increased by 9 places in the 2019 [Democracy Index](/wiki/Democracy_Index "Democracy Index") to 43rd compared to the previous year; the country is classified as a 'flawed democracy'.[[99]](#cite_note-106) Malaysia's ranking in the 2020 [Press Freedom Index](/wiki/Press_Freedom_Index "Press Freedom Index") increased by 22 places to 101st compared to the previous year, making it one of two countries in Southeast Asia without a 'Difficult situation' or 'Very Serious situation' with regards to press freedom.[[100]](#cite_note-107) However, it fell 18 places the following year due to the policies of the PN government.[[101]](#cite_note-108)

Malaysia is marked at 48th and 62nd place according to the 2021 [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index"), indicating above-average levels of [corruption](/wiki/Corruption_in_Malaysia "Corruption in Malaysia"). [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") noted Malaysia as "partly free" in its 2018 survey.[[102]](#cite_note-109) A lawsuit filed by the [Department of Justice](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") alleged that at least $3.5 billion involving former prime minister [Najib Razak](/wiki/Najib_Razak "Najib Razak") had been stolen from Malaysia's [1MDB](/wiki/1MDB "1MDB") state-owned fund, known as the [1Malaysia Development Berhad scandal](/wiki/1Malaysia_Development_Berhad_scandal "1Malaysia Development Berhad scandal").[[103]](#cite_note-:0-110)[[104]](#cite_note-111)[[105]](#cite_note-112) As of 2025, it is ranked 13th on the [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index").[[106]](#cite_note-113)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [States and federal territories of Malaysia](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia") and [Local government in Malaysia](/wiki/Local_government_in_Malaysia "Local government in Malaysia")

|  |
| --- |
| [Perlis](/wiki/Perlis "Perlis")  [Kedah](/wiki/Kedah "Kedah")  [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang")  [Kelantan](/wiki/Kelantan "Kelantan")  [Terengganu](/wiki/Terengganu "Terengganu")  [Perak](/wiki/Perak "Perak")  [Selangor](/wiki/Selangor "Selangor")  [Negeri Sembilan](/wiki/Negeri_Sembilan "Negeri Sembilan")  [Malacca](/wiki/Malacca "Malacca")  [Johor](/wiki/Johor "Johor")  [Pahang](/wiki/Pahang "Pahang")  [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak")  [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah")  [Labuan](/wiki/Labuan "Labuan")  [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur")  [Putrajaya](/wiki/Putrajaya "Putrajaya")  [West Malaysia](/wiki/West_Malaysia "West Malaysia")  [East Malaysia](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia")  [(Blue) States](/wiki/States_of_Malaysia "States of Malaysia")  [(Red) Federal Territories](/wiki/Federal_Territories_(Malaysia) "Federal Territories (Malaysia)")  [*South China Sea*](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea")  [*Strait  of  Malacca*](/wiki/Strait_of_Malacca "Strait of Malacca")  [*Gulf of Thailand*](/wiki/Gulf_of_Thailand "Gulf of Thailand")  [*Sulu Sea*](/wiki/Sulu_Sea "Sulu Sea")  [*Celebes Sea*](/wiki/Celebes_Sea "Celebes Sea")  [*Brunei*](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei")  [*Indonesia*](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")  [*Indonesia*](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")  [*Indonesia*](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia")  [*Philippines*](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines")  [*Singapore*](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")  [*Thailand*](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand") |

Malaysia is a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation") of [13 states and three federal territories](/wiki/States_and_federal_territories_of_Malaysia "States and federal territories of Malaysia").[[107]](#cite_note-114) Out of these, 11 states and two federal territories are in [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia"), whereas the other two states and one federal territory comprise [East Malaysia](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia").

The country has three tiers of government—[federal](/wiki/Government_of_Malaysia "Government of Malaysia"), [state](/wiki/State_governments_of_Malaysia "State governments of Malaysia"), and local.[[108]](#cite_note-:1-115) Governance of the states is divided between the federal and the state governments, with different powers reserved for each, and the federal government has direct administration of the federal territories.[[109]](#cite_note-116) Each state has a unicameral [State Legislative Assembly](/wiki/State_legislative_assemblies_of_Malaysia "State legislative assemblies of Malaysia") whose members are elected from single-member constituencies. State governments are led by [Chief Ministers](/wiki/Heads_of_state_governments_of_Malaysia "Heads of state governments of Malaysia"),[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) who are state assembly members from the majority party in the assembly. In each of the states with a hereditary ruler, the Chief Minister is normally required to be a [Malay](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)"), appointed by the ruler upon the recommendation of the [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia "Prime Minister of Malaysia").[[110]](#cite_note-117) Until [2018](/wiki/2018_Malaysian_general_election "2018 Malaysian general election"), state elections were held concurrently with the general election by [convention](/wiki/Constitutional_convention_(political_custom) "Constitutional convention (political custom)"), except for those in [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak").[[83]](#cite_note-dfat-90) Following the [2020–2022 political crisis](/wiki/2020%E2%80%932022_Malaysian_political_crisis "2020–2022 Malaysian political crisis"), only [Pahang](/wiki/Pahang "Pahang"), [Perak](/wiki/Perak "Perak") and [Perlis](/wiki/Perlis "Perlis") opted to conduct their state elections simultaneously with the general elections.[[111]](#cite_note-118)

Positioned below the federal and state governments, [local governments](/wiki/Local_government_in_Malaysia "Local government in Malaysia") represent the lowest tier of governance in Malaysia.[[108]](#cite_note-:1-115) As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Malaysia&action=edit), Malaysia is divided between 19 cities, 40 municipalities, 91 district-level councils, and four statutory agencies.[[112]](#cite_note-119) While the federal constitution assigns local authorities to the exclusive jurisdiction of state governments,[[113]](#cite_note-120) in practice, the [federal Ministry of Housing and Local Government](/wiki/Ministry_of_Housing_and_Local_Government_(Malaysia) "Ministry of Housing and Local Government (Malaysia)") oversees the regulation of local laws and policies.[[108]](#cite_note-:1-115)[[114]](#cite_note-121) [Mayors](/wiki/Mayor#Malaysia "Mayor") (or presidents for municipal and district councils) and [councillors](/wiki/Councillor "Councillor") are appointed by the respective state governments, or in the case of the federal territories, by the federal government.[[115]](#cite_note-:2-122)

Federal laws assign land matters, including the delineation of [districts](/wiki/List_of_districts_in_Malaysia "List of districts in Malaysia"), to the purview of state governments.[[116]](#cite_note-123) Except Perlis and the federal territories, each state is divided into districts, which are further subdivided into [mukims](/wiki/Mukim "Mukim"). In [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") and Sarawak, districts are grouped into divisions.[[117]](#cite_note-124) In contrast to local governments that manage municipal administration and infrastructure development,[[118]](#cite_note-125) districts are solely utilised for land taxation.[[119]](#cite_note-126)

The 13 states are based on historical Malay kingdoms, and 9 of the 11 Peninsular states, known as the [Malay states](/wiki/Malay_states "Malay states"), retain their royal families. [The King](/wiki/King_of_Malaysia "King of Malaysia") is elected by and from [the nine rulers](/wiki/Conference_of_Rulers "Conference of Rulers") to serve a five-year term.[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) This King appoints governors serving a four-year term for the states without monarchies, after consultations with the chief minister of that state. Each state has its written constitution.[[120]](#cite_note-Lim_Hong_Hai-127) Sabah and Sarawak have considerably more autonomy than the other states, most notably having separate immigration policies and controls, and unique residency status.[[121]](#cite_note-128)[[122]](#cite_note-129)[[123]](#cite_note-130) Federal intervention in state affairs, lack of development, and disputes over oil [royalties](/wiki/Royalties "Royalties") have occasionally led to statements about [secession](/wiki/Secession "Secession") from leaders in several states such as [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang"), [Johor](/wiki/Johor "Johor"), [Kelantan](/wiki/Kelantan "Kelantan"), Sabah and Sarawak, although these have not been followed up and no serious independence movements exist.[[124]](#cite_note-131)[[125]](#cite_note-132)[[126]](#cite_note-133)[[127]](#cite_note-134)
