### Foreign relations and military

Main articles: [Foreign relations of Malaysia](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Malaysia "Foreign relations of Malaysia") and [Malaysian Armed Forces](/wiki/Malaysian_Armed_Forces "Malaysian Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/2025_Donald_Trump%2C_Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia_Seri_Anwar_Ibrahim%2C_Prime_Minister_of_Cambodia_Hun_Manet_and_Prime_Minister_of_Thailand_Anutin_Charnvirakul_before_signing_the_Kuala_Lumpur_Accord_on_October_25_at_the_ASEAN_Summit.jpg/250px-thumbnail.jpg)](/wiki/File:2025_Donald_Trump,_Prime_Minister_of_Malaysia_Seri_Anwar_Ibrahim,_Prime_Minister_of_Cambodia_Hun_Manet_and_Prime_Minister_of_Thailand_Anutin_Charnvirakul_before_signing_the_Kuala_Lumpur_Accord_on_October_25_at_the_ASEAN_Summit.jpg)

(L-R) Malaysian Prime Minister [Anwar Ibrahim](/wiki/Anwar_Ibrahim "Anwar Ibrahim"), US President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), Thai Prime Minister [Anutin Charnvirakul](/wiki/Anutin_Charnvirakul "Anutin Charnvirakul") and Cambodian Prime Minister [Hun Manet](/wiki/Hun_Manet "Hun Manet") after signing the [Kuala Lumpur Peace Accord](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_Peace_Accord "Kuala Lumpur Peace Accord"), which took place during the [47th ASEAN Summit](/wiki/2025_ASEAN_Summits "2025 ASEAN Summits"), 26 October 2025

A founding member of [ASEAN](/wiki/ASEAN "ASEAN")[[128]](#cite_note-135) and [OIC](/wiki/OIC "OIC"),[[129]](#cite_note-136) the country participates in many international organisations such as the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (UN),[[130]](#cite_note-137) [APEC](/wiki/APEC "APEC"),[[131]](#cite_note-138) the [D-8 Organization for Economic Cooperation](/wiki/D-8_Organization_for_Economic_Cooperation "D-8 Organization for Economic Cooperation"),[[132]](#cite_note-139) and NAM.[[133]](#cite_note-140) It has chaired ASEAN, OIC, and NAM in the past.[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) A former [British colony](/wiki/British_colony "British colony"), it is also a member of the [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations").[[134]](#cite_note-141) [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur") was the site of the first [EAC](/wiki/East_Asia_Summit "East Asia Summit") in 2005.[[135]](#cite_note-govtnz-142)

Malaysia's [foreign policy](/wiki/Foreign_policy "Foreign policy") is officially based on the principle of neutrality and maintaining peaceful relations with all countries, regardless of their political system.[[136]](#cite_note-kln-143) The government attaches a high priority to the security and stability of [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"),[[135]](#cite_note-govtnz-142) and seeks to further develop relations with other countries in the region. Historically the government has tried to portray Malaysia as a progressive Islamic nation[[136]](#cite_note-kln-143) while strengthening relations with other [Islamic states](/wiki/Islamic_state "Islamic state").[[135]](#cite_note-govtnz-142) A strong tenet of Malaysia's policy is national sovereignty and right of a country to control its domestic affairs.[[83]](#cite_note-dfat-90) Malaysia signed the UN treaty on the [Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Prohibition_of_Nuclear_Weapons "Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons").[[137]](#cite_note-144)[[138]](#cite_note-145)

The [Spratly Islands](/wiki/Spratly_Islands "Spratly Islands") are disputed by many states in the area, and [a large portion of the South China Sea](/wiki/Territorial_disputes_in_the_South_China_Sea "Territorial disputes in the South China Sea") is claimed by [China](/wiki/China "China"). Unlike its neighbours of [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), Malaysia historically avoided conflicts with China.[[139]](#cite_note-146) However, after the encroachment of Chinese ships in Malaysian territorial waters,[[140]](#cite_note-147) and breach of airspace by their military aircraft, Malaysia has become active in condemning China.[[141]](#cite_note-148)[[142]](#cite_note-149) [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei") and Malaysia in 2009 announced an end to claims of each other's land, and committed to resolve issues related to their maritime borders.[[143]](#cite_note-150) The [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") has a [dormant claim](/wiki/North_Borneo_dispute "North Borneo dispute") to the eastern part of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah").[[144]](#cite_note-MYIDPHSG-151) [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore")'s land reclamation has caused tensions,[[145]](#cite_note-152) and minor maritime and land border disputes exist with [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").[[144]](#cite_note-MYIDPHSG-151)[[146]](#cite_note-153)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Su30mkm_flying_at_lima_two_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Su30mkm_flying_at_lima_two_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Su30mkm_flying_at_lima_two_(cropped).jpg)

[Sukhoi Su-30MKM](/wiki/Sukhoi_Su-30MKM "Sukhoi Su-30MKM") of the RMAF

The [Malaysian Armed Forces](/wiki/Malaysian_Armed_Forces "Malaysian Armed Forces") has three branches: the [Malaysian Army](/wiki/Malaysian_Army "Malaysian Army"), [Royal Malaysian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Malaysian_Navy "Royal Malaysian Navy") and the [Royal Malaysian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Malaysian_Air_Force "Royal Malaysian Air Force"). There is no conscription, and the required age for voluntary military service is 18. The military uses 1.5% of the country's GDP, and employs 1.23% of Malaysia's manpower.[[147]](#cite_note-154) Malaysian peacekeeping forces of [MALBATT](/wiki/MALBATT "MALBATT") have contributed to many U.N. peacekeeping missions, such as in [Congo](/wiki/United_Nations_Operation_in_the_Congo "United Nations Operation in the Congo"), [Iran–Iraq](/wiki/United_Nations_Iran%E2%80%93Iraq_Military_Observer_Group "United Nations Iran–Iraq Military Observer Group"), [Namibia](/wiki/United_Nations_Transition_Assistance_Group "United Nations Transition Assistance Group"), [Cambodia](/wiki/United_Nations_Transitional_Authority_in_Cambodia "United Nations Transitional Authority in Cambodia"), [Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/United_Nations_Protection_Force "United Nations Protection Force"), [Somalia](/wiki/United_Nations_Operation_in_Somalia_II "United Nations Operation in Somalia II"), [Kosovo](/wiki/United_Nations_Interim_Administration_Mission_in_Kosovo "United Nations Interim Administration Mission in Kosovo"), [East Timor](/wiki/United_Nations_Integrated_Mission_in_East_Timor "United Nations Integrated Mission in East Timor"), and [Lebanon](/wiki/United_Nations_Interim_Force_in_Lebanon "United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon").[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58)[[148]](#cite_note-155)

The [Five Power Defence Arrangements](/wiki/Five_Power_Defence_Arrangements "Five Power Defence Arrangements") is a regional security initiative that has been in place for almost 40 years. It involves joint military exercises held among Malaysia, Singapore, Australia, New Zealand, and the United Kingdom.[[149]](#cite_note-156) Joint exercises and war games have also been held with Brunei,[[150]](#cite_note-157) China,[[151]](#cite_note-158) India,[[152]](#cite_note-159) Indonesia,[[153]](#cite_note-160) Japan,[[154]](#cite_note-161) and the United States.[[155]](#cite_note-162) Malaysia, Philippines, Thailand, and Vietnam have agreed to host joint security force exercises to secure their maritime border and tackle issues such as illegal immigration, [piracy](/wiki/Piracy "Piracy"), and [smuggling](/wiki/Smuggling "Smuggling").[[156]](#cite_note-163)[[157]](#cite_note-164)[[158]](#cite_note-165) Previously there were fears that extremist militant activities in the Muslim areas of the [southern Philippines](/wiki/Moro_conflict_in_the_Philippines "Moro conflict in the Philippines")[[159]](#cite_note-166) and [southern Thailand](/wiki/South_Thailand_insurgency "South Thailand insurgency")[[160]](#cite_note-167) would spill over into Malaysia. Because of this, Malaysia began to increase its border security.[[161]](#cite_note-168)[[162]](#cite_note-169)

### Human rights

See also: [Human rights in Malaysia](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Malaysia "Human rights in Malaysia"), [LGBT rights in Malaysia](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Malaysia "LGBT rights in Malaysia"), and [Capital punishment in Malaysia](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_Malaysia "Capital punishment in Malaysia")

[Homosexuality](/wiki/Homosexuality "Homosexuality") is [illegal in Malaysia](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Malaysia "LGBT rights in Malaysia"),[[163]](#cite_note-170)[[164]](#cite_note-171) and authorities have imposed punishments such as [caning](/wiki/Caning_in_Malaysia "Caning in Malaysia") and imprisonment.[[165]](#cite_note-172)[[166]](#cite_note-173) [Human trafficking](/wiki/Human_trafficking "Human trafficking") and [sex trafficking in Malaysia](/wiki/Sex_trafficking_in_Malaysia "Sex trafficking in Malaysia") are significant problems.[[167]](#cite_note-174)[[168]](#cite_note-175) There have also been cases of vigilante executions and beatings against LGBT individuals in Malaysia.[[169]](#cite_note-176)[[170]](#cite_note-177) The illegality of homosexuality in Malaysia has also been the forefront of [Anwar Ibrahim](/wiki/Anwar_Ibrahim "Anwar Ibrahim")'s [sodomy trials](/wiki/Anwar_Ibrahim_sodomy_trials "Anwar Ibrahim sodomy trials"), which Anwar has called politically motivated, a characterization supported by the [Working Group on Arbitrary Detention](/wiki/Working_Group_on_Arbitrary_Detention "Working Group on Arbitrary Detention"), along with [Amnesty International](/wiki/Amnesty_International "Amnesty International") and the [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch").[[171]](#cite_note-178)[[172]](#cite_note-179)[[173]](#cite_note-180)

The [death penalty](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_Malaysia "Capital punishment in Malaysia") is in use for serious crimes such as [murder](/wiki/Murder "Murder"), [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism"), and [drug trafficking](/wiki/Drug_trafficking "Drug trafficking"), some of which were once mandatory.[[174]](#cite_note-181)[[175]](#cite_note-182) However, in July 2023, following the passing of the [Abolition of Mandatory Death Penalty Act 2023](/wiki/Abolition_of_Mandatory_Death_Penalty_Act_2023 "Abolition of Mandatory Death Penalty Act 2023"), mandatory death penalty was abolished for all crimes in Malaysia, thereby allowing judges to impose the capital punishment at their own discretion and on a case-by-case basis. The new law also abolished all [natural life imprisonment](/wiki/Life_imprisonment "Life imprisonment"), replacing it with 30 to 40 years prison terms instead.[[176]](#cite_note-183)[[177]](#cite_note-184)[[178]](#cite_note-185)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Malaysia](/wiki/Geography_of_Malaysia "Geography of Malaysia")

[![Relief map of Malaysia](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Malaysia_relief_location_map.jpg/500px-Malaysia_relief_location_map.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malaysia_relief_location_map.jpg)

[Topographic map](/wiki/Topographic_map "Topographic map") of Malaysia; [Mount Kinabalu](/wiki/Mount_Kinabalu "Mount Kinabalu") is the highest summit in the country.

Malaysia is the [66th largest country by total land area](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_outlying_territories_by_total_area "List of countries and outlying territories by total area"), with a total area of 330,803 km2 (127,724 sq mi).[[10]](#cite_note-2010_stats-15) [West Malaysia](/wiki/West_Malaysia "West Malaysia") shares [land borders](/wiki/Borders_of_Malaysia "Borders of Malaysia") with [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand "Thailand"), while [East Malaysia](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia") shares land borders with [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") and [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei").[[18]](#cite_note-CIA_Fact_Book-24) [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") is separated from the West by the [Straits of Johor](/wiki/Straits_of_Johor "Straits of Johor") and is linked to it by [a narrow causeway](/wiki/Johor%E2%80%93Singapore_Causeway "Johor–Singapore Causeway") and [a bridge](/wiki/Malaysia%E2%80%93Singapore_Second_Link "Malaysia–Singapore Second Link"). The country also shares [maritime boundaries](/wiki/Maritime_boundaries "Maritime boundaries") with Indonesia, [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam")[[179]](#cite_note-186) and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines").[[180]](#cite_note-187) The land borders are defined in large part by geological features such as the [Perlis River](/wiki/Perlis_River "Perlis River"), the [Golok River](/wiki/Golok_River "Golok River") and the Pagalayan Canal, whilst some of the maritime boundaries are the subject of ongoing contention.[[18]](#cite_note-CIA_Fact_Book-24) Brunei forms what is almost an [enclave](/wiki/Enclave "Enclave") in Malaysia,[[181]](#cite_note-188) with the state of [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") dividing it into two parts. Malaysia is the only country with territory on both the [Asian mainland](/wiki/Asia "Asia") and the [Malay Archipelago](/wiki/Malay_Archipelago "Malay Archipelago").[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) The [Strait of Malacca](/wiki/Strait_of_Malacca "Strait of Malacca"), lying between [Sumatra](/wiki/Sumatra "Sumatra") and Peninsular Malaysia, is one of the most important thoroughfares in global commerce, carrying 40% of the world's trade.[[183]](#cite_note-Schuman-190) The [southernmost point of mainland Asia](/wiki/Extreme_points_of_Asia "Extreme points of Asia") is located at [Tanjung Piai](/wiki/Tanjung_Piai "Tanjung Piai"), in the state of [Johor](/wiki/Johor "Johor").[[184]](#cite_note-191)

The two parts of Malaysia, separated from each other by the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea"), share a largely similar landscape in that both Peninsular and East Malaysia feature [coastal plains](/wiki/Coastal_plains "Coastal plains") rising to [hills](/wiki/Hills "Hills") and [mountains](/wiki/Mountains "Mountains").[[18]](#cite_note-CIA_Fact_Book-24) Peninsular Malaysia, containing 40% of Malaysia's land area,[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) extends 740 km (460 mi) from north to south, and its maximum width is 322 km (200 mi).[[185]](#cite_note-Swee-Hock-192) It is divided between its east and west coasts by the [Titiwangsa Mountains](/wiki/Titiwangsa_Mountains "Titiwangsa Mountains"),[[186]](#cite_note-193) rising to a peak elevation of 2,183 metres (7,162 ft) at [Mount Korbu](/wiki/Mount_Korbu "Mount Korbu"),[[187]](#cite_note-AZ-194) part of a series of [mountain ranges](/wiki/Mountain_range "Mountain range") running down the centre of the peninsula.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) These mountains are heavily [forested](/wiki/Forest "Forest"),[[188]](#cite_note-195) and mainly composed of [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") and other [igneous rocks](/wiki/Igneous_rock "Igneous rock"). Much of it has been eroded, creating a [karst](/wiki/Karst "Karst") landscape.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) The range is the origin of some of Peninsular Malaysia's river systems.[[189]](#cite_note-196) The coastal plains surrounding the peninsula reach a maximum width of 50 kilometres (31 mi), and the peninsula's coastline is nearly 1,931 km (1,200 mi) long, although [harbours](/wiki/Harbour "Harbour") are only available on the western side.[[185]](#cite_note-Swee-Hock-192)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/St._John%27s_Peak_Mount_Kinabalu_3.jpg/500px-St._John%27s_Peak_Mount_Kinabalu_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._John%27s_Peak_Mount_Kinabalu_3.jpg)

Climbers ascending toward Low's Peak before sunrise on Mount Kinabalu, with the distant city lights of Kota Kinabalu visible far right, Sabah, Borneo

East Malaysia, on the island of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo"), has a coastline of 2,607 km (1,620 mi).[[18]](#cite_note-CIA_Fact_Book-24) It is divided between coastal regions, hills and [valleys](/wiki/Valley "Valley"), and a mountainous interior.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) The [Crocker Range](/wiki/Crocker_Range "Crocker Range") extends northwards from Sarawak,[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) dividing the state of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah"). It is the location of the 4,095 m (13,435 ft) high [Mount Kinabalu](/wiki/Mount_Kinabalu "Mount Kinabalu"),[[190]](#cite_note-Richmond-197)[[191]](#cite_note-Thiessen-198) the [tallest mountain in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_mountains_in_Malaysia "List of mountains in Malaysia"). Mount Kinabalu is located in the [Kinabalu National Park](/wiki/Kinabalu_National_Park "Kinabalu National Park"), which is protected as one of the four [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") [in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Malaysia "List of World Heritage Sites in Malaysia").[[192]](#cite_note-199) The highest mountain ranges form the border between Malaysia and Indonesia. Sarawak contains the Mulu Caves, the largest cave system in the world, in the [Gunung Mulu National Park](/wiki/Gunung_Mulu_National_Park "Gunung Mulu National Park"), another World Heritage Site.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) Also in Sarawak is the [Rajang](/wiki/Rajang_River "Rajang River"), the [largest river in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_rivers_of_Malaysia "List of rivers of Malaysia").

Around the two halves of Malaysia are [numerous islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Malaysia "List of islands of Malaysia"), the largest of which is [Banggi](/wiki/Banggi "Banggi").[[193]](#cite_note-200) The local climate is [equatorial](/wiki/Tropical_climate "Tropical climate") and characterised by the annual southwest (April to October) and northeast (October to February) [monsoons](/wiki/Monsoon "Monsoon").[[185]](#cite_note-Swee-Hock-192) The temperature is moderated by the presence of the surrounding oceans.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) Humidity is usually high, and the average annual rainfall is 250 cm (98 in).[[185]](#cite_note-Swee-Hock-192) The climates of the Peninsula and the East differ, as the climate on the peninsula is directly affected by wind from the mainland, as opposed to the more maritime weather of the East. Local climates can be divided into three regions, highland, lowland, and coastal.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) [Climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_in_Malaysia "Climate change in Malaysia") will cause [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") and increased rainfall, increasing flood risks and leading to droughts.[[194]](#cite_note-201)

### Biodiversity and conservation

Main articles: [Wildlife of Malaysia](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Malaysia "Wildlife of Malaysia"), [Environmental issues in Malaysia](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_Malaysia "Environmental issues in Malaysia"), [Conservation in Malaysia](/wiki/Conservation_in_Malaysia "Conservation in Malaysia"), and [List of national parks in Malaysia](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_in_Malaysia "List of national parks in Malaysia")

[![Tiger resting on an artificial log platform](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/MalayanTiger.jpg/250px-MalayanTiger.jpg)](/wiki/File:MalayanTiger.jpg)

A [Malayan tiger](/wiki/Malayan_tiger "Malayan tiger") in the [National Zoo of Malaysia](/wiki/National_Zoo_of_Malaysia "National Zoo of Malaysia")

Malaysia signed the Rio [Convention on Biological Diversity](/wiki/Convention_on_Biological_Diversity "Convention on Biological Diversity") on 12 June 1993, and became a party to the convention on 24 June 1994.[[195]](#cite_note-202) It has subsequently produced a [National Biodiversity Strategy and Action Plan](/wiki/Biodiversity_action_plan "Biodiversity action plan"), which was received by the convention on 16 April 1998.[[196]](#cite_note-203) The country is [megadiverse](/wiki/Megadiverse "Megadiverse") with a high number of species and high levels of [endemism](/wiki/Endemism "Endemism").[[197]](#cite_note-megadiverse-204) It is estimated to contain 20% of the world's animal species.[[198]](#cite_note-Alexander-205) High levels of endemism are found on the diverse forests of [Borneo](/wiki/Borneo "Borneo")'s mountains, as species are isolated from each other by lowland forest.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189)

There are about 210 mammal species in the country.[[190]](#cite_note-Richmond-197) Over 620 species of birds have been recorded in [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia"),[[198]](#cite_note-Alexander-205) with many endemic to the mountains there. A high number of endemic bird species are also found in [Malaysian Borneo](/wiki/Malaysian_Borneo "Malaysian Borneo").[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) 250 [reptile](/wiki/Reptile "Reptile") species have been recorded in the country, with about 150 species of [snakes](/wiki/Snake "Snake")[[199]](#cite_note-Richmond2007-206) and 80 species of [lizards](/wiki/Lizard "Lizard").[[190]](#cite_note-Richmond-197) There are about 150 species of [frogs](/wiki/Frog "Frog"),[[190]](#cite_note-Richmond-197) and thousands of [insect](/wiki/Insect "Insect") species.[[190]](#cite_note-Richmond-197) The [Exclusive economic zone of Malaysia](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone_of_Malaysia "Exclusive economic zone of Malaysia") is 334,671 km2 (129,217 sq mi) and 1.5 times larger than its land area. It is mainly in the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea").[[200]](#cite_note-207)[[201]](#cite_note-208) Some of its waters are in the [Coral Triangle](/wiki/Coral_Triangle "Coral Triangle"), a biodiversity hotspot.[[202]](#cite_note-209) The waters around the island of [Sipadan](/wiki/Sipadan "Sipadan") are the most biodiverse in the world.[[198]](#cite_note-Alexander-205) Bordering East Malaysia, the [Sulu Sea](/wiki/Sulu_Sea "Sulu Sea") is a biodiversity hotspot, with around 600 [coral](/wiki/Coral "Coral") species and 1200 [fish](/wiki/Fish "Fish") species.[[203]](#cite_note-Gardeners-210) The unique biodiversity of Malaysian Caves always attracts lovers of [ecotourism](/wiki/Ecotourism "Ecotourism") from all over the world.[[204]](#cite_note-211)

Nearly 4,000 species of [fungi](/wiki/Fungi "Fungi"), including [lichen](/wiki/Lichen "Lichen")-forming species have been recorded from Malaysia. Of the two fungal groups with the largest number of species in Malaysia, the [Ascomycota](/wiki/Ascomycota "Ascomycota") and their asexual states have been surveyed in some habitats (decaying wood, marine, and freshwater ecosystems, as parasites of some plants, and as agents of biodegradation), but have not been or have been only poorly surveyed in other habitats (as endobionts, in soils, on dung, as human and animal pathogens); the [Basidiomycota](/wiki/Basidiomycota "Basidiomycota") are only partly surveyed: [bracket fungi](/wiki/Bracket_fungi "Bracket fungi"), mushrooms and toadstools have been studied, but Malaysian rust and smut fungi remain little known. Without a doubt, many more fungal species in Malaysia are yet to be recorded, and it is likely that many of those, when found, will be new to science.[[205]](#cite_note-212)

[![Red flower made of 5 petals surrounding a depressed centre, on the forest floor surrounded by dead leaves and small green plants](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/A_Rafflesia%2C_the_biggest_flower_in_the_world.jpg/250px-A_Rafflesia%2C_the_biggest_flower_in_the_world.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_Rafflesia,_the_biggest_flower_in_the_world.jpg)

Some species of *[Rafflesia](/wiki/Rafflesia "Rafflesia")* can grow up to 1 m (3 ft 3 in) in diameter, making them the largest flowers in the world.

About two-thirds of Malaysia was covered in [forest](/wiki/Forest "Forest") as of 2007,[[185]](#cite_note-Swee-Hock-192) with some forests believed to be 130 million years old.[[190]](#cite_note-Richmond-197) The forests are dominated by [dipterocarps](/wiki/Dipterocarpaceae "Dipterocarpaceae").[[206]](#cite_note-WWF-213) Lowland forest covers areas below 760 m (2,490 ft),[[185]](#cite_note-Swee-Hock-192) and formerly East Malaysia was covered in [such rainforest](/wiki/Borneo_lowland_rain_forests "Borneo lowland rain forests"),[[206]](#cite_note-WWF-213) which is supported by its hot wet climate.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) There are around 14,500 species of [flowering plants](/wiki/Flowering_plant "Flowering plant") and [trees](/wiki/Tree "Tree").[[190]](#cite_note-Richmond-197) Besides [rainforests](/wiki/Rainforest "Rainforest"), there are over 1,425 km2 (550 sq mi) of [mangroves](/wiki/Mangroves "Mangroves") in Malaysia,[[185]](#cite_note-Swee-Hock-192) and a large amount of peat forest. At higher altitudes, [oaks](/wiki/Oak "Oak"), [chestnuts](/wiki/Chestnut "Chestnut"), and [rhododendrons](/wiki/Rhododendron "Rhododendron") replace [dipterocarps](/wiki/Dipterocarpaceae "Dipterocarpaceae").[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) There are an estimated 8,500 species of [vascular plants](/wiki/Vascular_plant "Vascular plant") in Peninsular Malaysia, with another 15,000 in the East.[[207]](#cite_note-214) The forests of East Malaysia are estimated to be the habitat of around 2,000 tree species, and are one of the most biodiverse areas in the world, with 240 different species of trees every hectare.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) These forests host many members of the *[Rafflesia](/wiki/Rafflesia "Rafflesia")* genus, the largest flowers in the world,[[206]](#cite_note-WWF-213) with a maximum diameter of 1 m (3 ft 3 in).[[208]](#cite_note-Richmond2-215)

[Logging](/wiki/Logging "Logging"), along with cultivation practices, has devastated tree cover, causing severe environmental degradation in the country. Over 80% of [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak")'s rainforest has been logged.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) Floods in East Malaysia have been worsened by the loss of trees, and over 60% of the peninsula's forests have been cleared.[[208]](#cite_note-Richmond2-215) With current rates of [deforestation](/wiki/Social_and_environmental_impact_of_palm_oil#Deforestation "Social and environmental impact of palm oil"), mainly for the [palm oil](/wiki/Palm_oil_production_in_Malaysia "Palm oil production in Malaysia") industry, the forests are predicted to be extinct by 2020.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189)[[209]](#cite_note-216) [Deforestation](/wiki/Deforestation_in_Malaysia "Deforestation in Malaysia") is a major problem for animals, fungi and plants, having caused species such as *[Begonia eiromischa](/wiki/Begonia_eiromischa "Begonia eiromischa")* to go extinct.[[210]](#cite_note-Tigers-217) Most remaining forest is found inside reserves and national parks.[[208]](#cite_note-Richmond2-215) Habitat destruction has proved a threat for [marine life](/wiki/Marine_life "Marine life").[[203]](#cite_note-Gardeners-210) [Illegal fishing](/wiki/Illegal_fishing "Illegal fishing") is another major threat,[[203]](#cite_note-Gardeners-210) with fishing methods such as [dynamite fishing](/wiki/Dynamite_fishing "Dynamite fishing") and poisoning depleting marine ecosystems.[[211]](#cite_note-Artificial-218) [Leatherback turtle](/wiki/Leatherback_turtle "Leatherback turtle") numbers have dropped by 98% since the 1950s.[[199]](#cite_note-Richmond2007-206) Hunting has also been an issue for some animals,[[208]](#cite_note-Richmond2-215) with [overconsumption](/wiki/Overconsumption "Overconsumption") and the use of animal parts for profit endangering many animals, from marine life[[203]](#cite_note-Gardeners-210) to [tigers](/wiki/Tiger "Tiger").[[210]](#cite_note-Tigers-217) Marine life is also detrimentally affected by uncontrolled tourism.[[212]](#cite_note-Go-219)

The [Malaysian government](/wiki/Malaysian_government "Malaysian government") aims to balance economic growth with environmental protection, but has been accused of favouring big business over the environment.[[208]](#cite_note-Richmond2-215) Some state governments are now trying to counter the environmental impact and pollution created by deforestation;[[206]](#cite_note-WWF-213) and the federal government is trying to cut logging by 10% each year. A total of [28 national parks](/wiki/List_of_national_parks_of_Malaysia "List of national parks of Malaysia") have been established, 23 in East Malaysia and five in the peninsula.[[208]](#cite_note-Richmond2-215) Tourism has been limited in biodiverse areas such as the island of Sipadan.[[212]](#cite_note-Go-219) [Wildlife trafficking](/wiki/Wildlife_trafficking "Wildlife trafficking") is a large issue, and the Malaysian government has held talks with the governments of [Brunei](/wiki/Government_of_Brunei "Government of Brunei") and [Indonesia](/wiki/Government_of_Indonesia "Government of Indonesia") to standardise anti-trafficking laws.[[213]](#cite_note-220)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Malaysia](/wiki/Economy_of_Malaysia "Economy of Malaysia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/GDP_per_capita_development_of_Malaysia.svg/330px-GDP_per_capita_development_of_Malaysia.svg.png)](/wiki/File:GDP_per_capita_development_of_Malaysia.svg)

Development of real GDP per capita, 1820 to 2018

Malaysia is a relatively [open](/wiki/Open_economy "Open economy") state-oriented and [newly industrialised](/wiki/Newly_industrialised_country "Newly industrialised country") [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy").[[214]](#cite_note-221) It has the world's [36th-largest economy](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") by nominal GDP and the [31st-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP) "List of countries by GDP (PPP)") by [PPP](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity"). In 2017, the large [service sector](/wiki/Service_sector "Service sector") contributed to 53.6% of total [GDP](/wiki/GDP "GDP"), the industrial sector 37.6%, and the small agricultural sector roughly 8.8%.[[215]](#cite_note-222) Malaysia has a low official [unemployment rate](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_unemployment_rate "List of countries by unemployment rate") of 3.4% as of 2024.[[216]](#cite_note-223) Its foreign exchange reserves are the world's [24th-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_foreign-exchange_reserves "List of countries by foreign-exchange reserves").[[217]](#cite_note-224) It has a labour force of about 15 million, which is the world's [34th-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_labour_force "List of countries by labour force").[[218]](#cite_note-225) Malaysia's large [automotive industry](/wiki/Automotive_industry_in_Malaysia "Automotive industry in Malaysia") ranks as the world's [22nd-largest by production](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_motor_vehicle_production "List of countries by motor vehicle production").[[219]](#cite_note-226)

Malaysia is the world's 23rd-largest [exporter](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_exports "List of countries by exports") and 25th-largest [importer](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_imports "List of countries by imports").[[220]](#cite_note-227)[[221]](#cite_note-228) However, economic inequalities exist between different ethnic groups.[[222]](#cite_note-229) The Chinese make up about one-quarter of the population, but account for 70% of the country's market capitalisation.[[223]](#cite_note-230) Chinese businesses in Malaysia are part of the larger [bamboo network](/wiki/Bamboo_network "Bamboo network"), a network of [overseas Chinese](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese") businesses in the Southeast Asian market sharing common family and cultural ties.[[224]](#cite_note-Weidenbaum-231)

International trade, facilitated by the shipping route in adjacent [Strait of Malacca](/wiki/Strait_of_Malacca "Strait of Malacca"), and manufacturing are the key sectors.[[225]](#cite_note-232)[[226]](#cite_note-BNM-233)[[183]](#cite_note-Schuman-190) Malaysia is an exporter of natural and agricultural resources, and petroleum is a major export.[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) Malaysia has once been the largest producer of [tin](/wiki/Tin "Tin"),[[227]](#cite_note-234) [rubber](/wiki/Rubber "Rubber") and [palm oil](/wiki/Palm_oil "Palm oil") in the world. Manufacturing has a large influence in the country's economy,[[226]](#cite_note-BNM-233) although Malaysia's economic structure has been moving away from it.[[228]](#cite_note-WPRO-235) Malaysia remains one of the world's largest producers of palm oil.[[229]](#cite_note-236)

[Tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Malaysia "Tourism in Malaysia") is the third-largest contributor to Malaysia's GDP, after the manufacturing and commodities sectors.[[230]](#cite_note-237) In 2019, the sector contributed about 15.9% to the total GDP. According to the [World Tourism Organization](/wiki/World_Tourism_Organization "World Tourism Organization"), Malaysia was the fourteenth-most visited country in the world, and the fourth-most visited country in Asia in 2019, with over 26.1 million visits.[[231]](#cite_note-tourism-238) Malaysia was ranked 38th in the Travel and Tourism Competitiveness Report 2019.[[232]](#cite_note-239) Its international tourism receipts in 2019 amounted to $19.8 billion.[[231]](#cite_note-tourism-238)

The country has developed into a centre of [Islamic banking](/wiki/Islamic_banking "Islamic banking") and has the highest number of female workers in that industry.[[233]](#cite_note-iht001-240) Knowledge-based services are also expanding.[[228]](#cite_note-WPRO-235) In 2020, Malaysia exported high-tech products worth $92.1 billion, the second-highest in ASEAN, after Singapore.[[234]](#cite_note-241) Malaysia was ranked 34th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025[[235]](#cite_note-242)[[236]](#cite_note-243), and 32nd in the [Global Competitiveness Report](/wiki/Global_Competitiveness_Report "Global Competitiveness Report") in 2022.[[237]](#cite_note-244)

### Infrastructure

See also: [Transport in Malaysia](/wiki/Transport_in_Malaysia "Transport in Malaysia")

[![Expressway](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/NorthSouth-Expressway.jpg/250px-NorthSouth-Expressway.jpg)](/wiki/File:NorthSouth-Expressway.jpg)

[North–South Expressway](/wiki/North%E2%80%93South_Expressway_(Malaysia) "North–South Expressway (Malaysia)")

[![Airport](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/KLIA_MTB%26Tower.jpg/250px-KLIA_MTB%26Tower.jpg)](/wiki/File:KLIA_MTB%26Tower.jpg)

[Kuala Lumpur International Airport](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_International_Airport "Kuala Lumpur International Airport")

[Railway transport in Malaysia](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Malaysia "Rail transport in Malaysia") is state-run, and spans some 2,783 kilometres (1,729 mi).[[238]](#cite_note-245) As of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Malaysia&action=edit), Malaysia has the world's [26th-largest road network](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_road_network_size "List of countries by road network size"), with some 238,823 kilometres (148,398 mi) of [roads](/wiki/Road "Road"). Malaysia's inland [waterways](/wiki/Waterway "Waterway") are the world's [22nd-longest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_waterways_length "List of countries by waterways length"), and total 7,200 km (4,474 mi).[[239]](#cite_note-246) Among [Malaysia's 114 airports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Malaysia "List of airports in Malaysia"),[[240]](#cite_note-247) the [busiest one](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Malaysia "List of the busiest airports in Malaysia") is [Kuala Lumpur International Airport](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur_International_Airport "Kuala Lumpur International Airport") (KLIA), located in the [Sepang District](/wiki/Sepang_District "Sepang District"); it is also the [12th-busiest airport in Asia](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Asia "List of the busiest airports in Asia"). Among the seven federal [ports](/wiki/Port "Port"), the major one is [Port Klang](/wiki/Port_Klang "Port Klang"),[[241]](#cite_note-248) which is the [13th-busiest container port](/wiki/List_of_busiest_container_ports "List of busiest container ports").[[242]](#cite_note-249) Malaysia's [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") is [Malaysia Airlines](/wiki/Malaysia_Airlines "Malaysia Airlines"), providing international and domestic air services.[[243]](#cite_note-250)

[Malaysia's telecommunications](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Malaysia "Telecommunications in Malaysia") network is second only to [Singapore](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_Singapore "Telecommunications in Singapore")'s in [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia"), with 4.7 million fixed-line subscribers and more than 30 million cellular subscribers.[[244]](#cite_note-Tele-251)[[245]](#cite_note-252) There are 200 [industrial parks](/wiki/Industrial_park "Industrial park") along with specialised parks such as [Technology Park Malaysia](/wiki/Technology_Park_Malaysia "Technology Park Malaysia") and [Kulim Hi-Tech Park](/wiki/Kulim_Hi-Tech_Park "Kulim Hi-Tech Park").[[246]](#cite_note-MIDA-253) [Fresh water](/wiki/Fresh_water "Fresh water") is available to over 95% of the population, with [groundwater](/wiki/Groundwater "Groundwater") accounting for 90% of the freshwater resources.[[247]](#cite_note-254)[[248]](#cite_note-255) Although rural areas have been the focus of great development, they still lag behind areas such as the West Coast of [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia").[[249]](#cite_note-256) The [telecommunication](/wiki/Telecommunication "Telecommunication") network, although strong in [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area"), is less available to the [rural](/wiki/Rural "Rural") population.[[244]](#cite_note-Tele-251)

[Malaysia's energy](/wiki/Energy_policy_of_Malaysia "Energy policy of Malaysia") infrastructure sector is largely dominated by [Tenaga Nasional](/wiki/Tenaga_Nasional "Tenaga Nasional"), the largest [electric utility](/wiki/Electric_utility "Electric utility") company in Southeast Asia. Customers in Peninsular Malaysia are connected to electricity through the [National Grid](/wiki/National_Grid_(Malaysia) "National Grid (Malaysia)").[[250]](#cite_note-257) The other two electric utility companies in the country are [Sarawak Energy](/wiki/Sarawak_Energy "Sarawak Energy") and [Sabah Electricity](/wiki/Sabah_Electricity "Sabah Electricity").[[251]](#cite_note-258) In 2013, Malaysia's total power generation capacity was over 29,728 [megawatts](/wiki/Megawatts "Megawatts"). Total electricity generation was 140,985.01 [GWh](/wiki/GWh "GWh") and total electricity consumption was 116,087.51 GWh.[[252]](#cite_note-259) [Energy production](/wiki/List_of_power_stations_in_Malaysia "List of power stations in Malaysia") in Malaysia is largely based on oil and natural gas, owing to Malaysia's oil and natural gas reserves, which are the fourth largest in the [Asia-Pacific](/wiki/Asia-Pacific "Asia-Pacific") region.[[253]](#cite_note-Unimap-260)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Malaysia](/wiki/Demographics_of_Malaysia "Demographics of Malaysia")

[![A map of Malaysia depicting the expected 2010 estimated population density.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Malaysia_population_density_2010b.png/500px-Malaysia_population_density_2010b.png)](/wiki/File:Malaysia_population_density_2010b.png)

Population density (person per km2) in 2010

 [![Percentage distribution of Malaysian population by ethnic group, 2010](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/Percentage_distribution_of_Malaysian_population_by_ethnic_group%2C_2010.svg/500px-Percentage_distribution_of_Malaysian_population_by_ethnic_group%2C_2010.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Percentage_distribution_of_Malaysian_population_by_ethnic_group,_2010.svg)

The percentage distribution of Malaysian population by ethnic group based on 2010 census

According to the Malaysian Department of Statistics, the country's population was 32,447,385 in 2020,[[254]](#cite_note-Population2010-261) making it the [42nd-most populated country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_population_in_2010 "List of countries by population in 2010"). According to a 2012 estimate, the population is increasing by 1.54% per year. Malaysia has an average population density of 96 people per km2, ranking it [116th in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_and_territories_by_population_density "List of countries and territories by population density") for population density. People within the 15–64 [age group](/wiki/Age_group "Age group") constitute 69.5% of the total population; the 0–14 age group corresponds to 24.5%; while senior citizens aged 65 years or older make up 6.0%. In 1960, when the first official census was recorded in Malaysia, the population was 8.11 million. 91.8% of the population are [Malaysian citizens](/wiki/Malaysians "Malaysians").[[255]](#cite_note-stat2010-262)

Malaysian citizens are divided along local ethnic lines, with 69.7% considered *[bumiputera](/wiki/Bumiputera_(Malaysia) "Bumiputera (Malaysia)")*.[[256]](#cite_note-Q12021-263) The largest group of bumiputera are [Malays](/wiki/Malays_(ethnic_group) "Malays (ethnic group)"), who are defined in the constitution as [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") who practise Malay customs and culture. They play a dominant role politically.[[257]](#cite_note-264) Bumiputera status is also accorded to the non-Malay indigenous groups of Sabah and Sarawak: which includes [Dayaks](/wiki/Dayaks "Dayaks") ([Iban](/wiki/Iban_people "Iban people"), [Bidayuh](/wiki/Bidayuh "Bidayuh"), [Orang Ulu](/wiki/Orang_Ulu "Orang Ulu")), [Kadazan-Dusun](/wiki/Kadazan-Dusun "Kadazan-Dusun"), [Melanau](/wiki/Melanau "Melanau"), [Bajau](/wiki/Bajau "Bajau") and others. Non-Malay bumiputeras make up more than half of [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak")'s population and over two-thirds of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah")'s population.[[258]](#cite_note-265)[[259]](#cite_note-266) There are also indigenous or aboriginal groups in much smaller numbers on the peninsula, where they are collectively known as the [Orang Asli](/wiki/Orang_Asli "Orang Asli").[[260]](#cite_note-267) Laws over who receives bumiputera status vary between states.[[261]](#cite_note-268)

There are also two other non-Bumiputera local ethnic groups. 22.8% of the population are [Malaysian Chinese](/wiki/Malaysian_Chinese "Malaysian Chinese"), while 6.8% are [Malaysian Indian](/wiki/Malaysian_Indian "Malaysian Indian").[[256]](#cite_note-Q12021-263) The local Chinese have historically been more dominant in the business community. Local Indians are mostly of [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_people "Tamil people") descent.[[262]](#cite_note-269)[[263]](#cite_note-Barbara-270) [Malaysian citizenship](/wiki/Malaysian_citizenship "Malaysian citizenship") is not automatically granted to those born in Malaysia, but is granted to a child born of two Malaysian parents outside Malaysia. [Dual citizenship](/wiki/Dual_citizenship "Dual citizenship") is not permitted.[[264]](#cite_note-271) Citizenship in the states of Sabah and Sarawak in [Malaysian Borneo](/wiki/Malaysian_Borneo "Malaysian Borneo") are distinct from citizenship in [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia") for immigration purposes. Every citizen is issued a biometric smart chip [identity card](/wiki/Identity_card "Identity card") known as *[MyKad](/wiki/MyKad "MyKad")* at the age of 12 and must carry the card at all times.[[265]](#cite_note-272)

The population is concentrated on Peninsular Malaysia,[[266]](#cite_note-273) where 20 million out of approximately 28 million Malaysians live.[[51]](#cite_note-state.gov-58) 70% of the population is urban.[[18]](#cite_note-CIA_Fact_Book-24) Due to the rise in labour-intensive industries,[[267]](#cite_note-274) the country is estimated to have over 3 million migrant workers; about 10% of the population.[[268]](#cite_note-275) Sabah-based NGOs estimate that out of the 3 million that make up the population of Sabah, 2 million are illegal immigrants.[[269]](#cite_note-276) Malaysia hosts a population of refugees and asylum seekers numbering approximately 171,500. Of this population, approximately 79,000 are from [Myanmar](/wiki/Myanmar "Myanmar"), 72,400 from the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), and 17,700 from [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"). Malaysian officials are reported to have turned deportees directly over to human smugglers in 2007, and Malaysia employs [RELA](/wiki/RELA_Corps "RELA Corps"), a volunteer militia with a history of controversies, to enforce its immigration law.[[270]](#cite_note-277)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Malaysia](/wiki/Religion_in_Malaysia "Religion in Malaysia")

[![A Map of Malaysia showing religious statistics by state](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/84/Malaysia_religion_by_state_2020.jpg/250px-Malaysia_religion_by_state_2020.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malaysia_religion_by_state_2020.jpg)

Dominant religious confessions in Malaysia according to [2020 census](/wiki/Census_in_Malaysia "Census in Malaysia"):[[271]](#cite_note-278)   
 Dark green: Muslim majority > 50%   
 Light green: Muslim plurality < 50%   
 Blue: Christian majority > 50%

The constitution grants freedom of religion, while establishing Islam as the "religion of the Federation".[[272]](#cite_note-279)[[273]](#cite_note-malaysianbar.org.my-280) According to the Population and Housing Census 2020 figures, ethnicity and religious beliefs correlate highly. Approximately 63.5% of the population practise [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Malaysia "Islam in Malaysia"), 18.7% practise [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Malaysia "Buddhism in Malaysia"), 9.1% [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Malaysia "Christianity in Malaysia"), 6.1% [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Malaysia "Hinduism in Malaysia") and 1.3% practise [Confucianism](/wiki/Confucianism "Confucianism"), [Taoism](/wiki/Taoism "Taoism") and other traditional [Chinese religions](/wiki/Chinese_folk_religion "Chinese folk religion"). 2.7% declared no religion or practised other religions or did not provide any information.[[254]](#cite_note-Population2010-261) The states of [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") and [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang"), as well as the federal territory of [Kuala Lumpur](/wiki/Kuala_Lumpur "Kuala Lumpur") have non-Muslim majorities.[[274]](#cite_note-281)[[275]](#cite_note-282)

[Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam") of the [Shafi'i](/wiki/Shafi%27i "Shafi'i") school of [jurisprudence](/wiki/Jurisprudence "Jurisprudence") is the dominant branch of [Islam in Malaysia](/wiki/Islam_in_Malaysia "Islam in Malaysia"),[[276]](#cite_note-Peletz2002-283)[[277]](#cite_note-284) while 18% are [nondenominational Muslims](/wiki/Nondenominational_Muslims "Nondenominational Muslims").[[278]](#cite_note-285) The Malaysian constitution strictly defines what makes a "Malay", defining Malays as those who are Muslim, speak Malay regularly, practise Malay customs, and lived in or have ancestors from [Brunei](/wiki/Religion_in_Brunei "Religion in Brunei"), Malaysia and [Singapore](/wiki/Religion_in_Singapore "Religion in Singapore").[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) Statistics from the 2010 Census indicate that 83.6% of the Chinese population identify as Buddhist, with significant numbers of adherents following Taoism (3.4%) and Christianity (11.1%), along with small Muslim populations in areas like Penang. The majority of the Indian population follows Hinduism (86.2%), with a significant minority identifying as Christians (6.0%) or Muslims (4.1%). Christianity is the predominant religion of the non-Malay *bumiputera* community (46.5%), while 40.4% identify as Muslims.[[254]](#cite_note-Population2010-261)

[Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims") are obliged to follow the decisions of [Syariah Courts](/wiki/Syariah_Court "Syariah Court") (i.e. Shariah courts) in matters concerning their religion. The Islamic judges are expected to follow the Shafi'i legal school of Islam, which is the main *[madhhab](/wiki/Madhhab "Madhhab")* ('school of thought') of Malaysia.[[276]](#cite_note-Peletz2002-283) The jurisdiction of Syariah courts is limited to Muslims in matters such as [marriage](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage"), [inheritance](/wiki/Inheritance "Inheritance"), [divorce](/wiki/Divorce "Divorce"), [apostasy](/wiki/Apostasy "Apostasy"), [religious conversion](/wiki/Religious_conversion "Religious conversion"), custody, and limited Islamic criminal law. No other criminal or civil offences are under the jurisdiction of the Syariah courts, which have a similar hierarchy to the [civil courts](/wiki/Courts_of_Malaysia "Courts of Malaysia"). The civil courts do not hear matters related to Islamic practices.[[279]](#cite_note-286)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Malaysia](/wiki/Languages_of_Malaysia "Languages of Malaysia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/Distribution_of_Malaysia_Families_Languages.png/250px-Distribution_of_Malaysia_Families_Languages.png)](/wiki/File:Distribution_of_Malaysia_Families_Languages.png)

The distribution of language families of Malaysia shown by colours:

[Malayic](/wiki/Malayic_languages "Malayic languages")

[Bornean](/wiki/Bornean_languages "Bornean languages")

[Aslian](/wiki/Aslian "Aslian")

[Land Dayak](/wiki/Land_Dayak "Land Dayak")

[Sama–Bajaw](/wiki/Sama%E2%80%93Bajaw_languages "Sama–Bajaw languages")

[Philippine](/wiki/Philippine_languages "Philippine languages")

[Chinese](/wiki/Varieties_of_Chinese "Varieties of Chinese")

Areas with multiple languages

The official and national language of Malaysia is [Malaysian Malay](/wiki/Malaysian_Malay "Malaysian Malay"),[[18]](#cite_note-CIA_Fact_Book-24) a standardised form of the [Malay language](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language").[[280]](#cite_note-287) The previous official terminology was *Bahasa Malaysia* (lit. 'Malaysian language')[[281]](#cite_note-288)[[282]](#cite_note-289)[[283]](#cite_note-290) but now government policy uses *Bahasa Melayu* (Malay language) to refer to the official language[[284]](#cite_note-291) and both terms remain in use.[[285]](#cite_note-292)[[286]](#cite_note-293) The National Language Act 1967 specifies the [Latin (Rumi) script](/wiki/Malay_alphabet "Malay alphabet") as the official [script](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") of the national language, but does not prohibit the use of the traditional [Jawi script](/wiki/Jawi_script "Jawi script").[[287]](#cite_note-National_Language_Act_1967-294)

[English](/wiki/English_language "English language") remains an active [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language"), with its use allowed for some official purposes under the National Language Act of 1967.[[287]](#cite_note-National_Language_Act_1967-294) In [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak"), English is an official state language alongside Malay.[[288]](#cite_note-lang1-295)[[289]](#cite_note-lang2-296)[[290]](#cite_note-lang3-297) Historically, English was the de facto administrative language; Malay became predominant after the 1969 race riots ([13 May incident](/wiki/13_May_incident "13 May incident")).[[291]](#cite_note-History-298) [Malaysian English](/wiki/Malaysian_English "Malaysian English"), also known as Malaysian Standard English, is a form of English derived from [British English](/wiki/British_English "British English"). Malaysian English is widely used in business, along with [Manglish](/wiki/Manglish "Manglish"), which is a colloquial form of English with heavy Malay, [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"), and [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") influences. The government discourages the use of non-standard Malay but has no power to issue compounds or fines to those who use what is perceived as improper Malay on their advertisements.[[292]](#cite_note-299)[[293]](#cite_note-300)

Malaysia is also home to [111 living indigenous languages](/wiki/Languages_of_Malaysia "Languages of Malaysia").[[294]](#cite_note-301) Some Orang Asli languages are now extinct, such the Low Country [Semang](/wiki/Semang "Semang") dialects once spoken in Penang.[[295]](#cite_note-302) Many other languages are used in Malaysia, which contains speakers of 137 living languages.[[296]](#cite_note-303) [Peninsular Malaysia](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia") contains speakers of 41 of these languages.[[297]](#cite_note-304) The native tribes of [East Malaysia](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia") have their own languages which are related to, but easily distinguishable from, Malay. [Iban](/wiki/Iban_language "Iban language") is the main tribal language in Sarawak while [Dusunic](/wiki/Dusunic_languages "Dusunic languages") and [Kadazan](/wiki/Coastal_Kadazan_language "Coastal Kadazan language") languages are spoken by the natives in [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah").[[298]](#cite_note-305) [Chinese Malaysians](/wiki/Chinese_Malaysians "Chinese Malaysians") predominantly speak Chinese dialects from the [southern part](/wiki/South_China "South China") of [China](/wiki/China "China"). The more common [Chinese varieties](/wiki/Chinese_varieties "Chinese varieties") in the country are [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese"), [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese"), [Hokkien](/wiki/Hokkien "Hokkien"), and so on.[[299]](#cite_note-306) The [Tamil language](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") is used predominantly by the majority of [Malaysian Indians](/wiki/Malaysian_Indians "Malaysian Indians").[[300]](#cite_note-307) A small number of Malaysians have [European ancestry](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe") and speak [creole languages](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language"), such as the Portuguese-based [Malaccan Creoles](/wiki/Portuguese-based_creole_languages#Southeast_Asia "Portuguese-based creole languages"),[[301]](#cite_note-308) and the Spanish-based [Chavacano language](/wiki/Chavacano_language "Chavacano language").[[302]](#cite_note-309)

### Health

Main articles: [Health in Malaysia](/wiki/Health_in_Malaysia "Health in Malaysia") and [Healthcare in Malaysia](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Malaysia "Healthcare in Malaysia")

Malaysia operates an efficient and widespread [two-tier healthcare](/wiki/Two-tier_healthcare "Two-tier healthcare") system, consisting of a [universal healthcare](/wiki/Universal_healthcare "Universal healthcare") system and a co-existing private healthcare system; provided by highly subsidized healthcare through its extensive network of public hospitals and clinics.[[303]](#cite_note-310) The [Ministry of Health](/wiki/Ministry_of_Health_(Malaysia) "Ministry of Health (Malaysia)") is the main provider of healthcare services to the country's population.[[304]](#cite_note-311) Malaysia's healthcare system is considered to be among the most developed in [Asia](/wiki/Health_care_systems_by_country#Asia "Health care systems by country"), which contributes to its thriving [medical tourism industry](/wiki/Medical_tourism_in_Malaysia "Medical tourism in Malaysia").[[305]](#cite_note-312)

Malaysia spent 3.83% of its GDP on healthcare in 2019.[[306]](#cite_note-313) In 2020, the overall [life expectancy](/wiki/Life_expectancy "Life expectancy") in Malaysia at birth was 76 years (74 years for males and 78 years for females),[[307]](#cite_note-314) and it had an [infant mortality rate](/wiki/Infant_mortality_rate "Infant mortality rate") of 7 deaths per 1000 births.[[308]](#cite_note-315) Malaysia had a [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") of 2.0 in 2020, which is just below the [replacement level](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility "Sub-replacement fertility") of 2.1.[[309]](#cite_note-316) In 2020, the country's crude [birth rate](/wiki/Birth_rate "Birth rate") was 16 per 1000 people, and the crude [death rate](/wiki/Death_rate "Death rate") was 5 per 1000 people.[[310]](#cite_note-317)[[311]](#cite_note-318)

In 2021, the principal cause of death among Malaysian adults was [coronary artery disease](/wiki/Coronary_artery_disease "Coronary artery disease"), representing 17% of the medically certified deaths in 2020 – being followed by [pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia"); which accounted for 11% of the deaths.[[312]](#cite_note-319) [Transport accidents](/wiki/Transport_accidents "Transport accidents") are considered a major health hazard, as Malaysia, relative to its population, has one of the [highest traffic fatality rates](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_traffic-related_death_rate "List of countries by traffic-related death rate") in the world.[[313]](#cite_note-320) [Smoking](/wiki/Smoking_in_Malaysia "Smoking in Malaysia") is also considered a major health issue across the country.[[314]](#cite_note-321)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Malaysia](/wiki/Education_in_Malaysia "Education in Malaysia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Putrajaya_Malaysia_Ministry-of-Education-03.jpg/250px-Putrajaya_Malaysia_Ministry-of-Education-03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Putrajaya_Malaysia_Ministry-of-Education-03.jpg)

[Ministry of Education](/wiki/Ministry_of_Education_(Malaysia) "Ministry of Education (Malaysia)"), Putrajaya

The education system of Malaysia features a non-compulsory kindergarten education followed by six years of compulsory primary education and five years of optional secondary education.[[315]](#cite_note-322) Schools in the primary education system are divided into two categories: national primary schools, which teach in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") (i.e.:[Sekolah Kebangsaan Sungai Binjai](/wiki/Sekolah_Kebangsaan_Sungai_Binjai "Sekolah Kebangsaan Sungai Binjai"), [SK Bukit Tiu](/wiki/SK_Bukit_Tiu "SK Bukit Tiu")), and vernacular schools, which teach in [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") or [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") (i.e.:[SJK(C) Pin Hwa 2](/wiki/SJK(C)_Pin_Hwa_2 "SJK(C) Pin Hwa 2"), SJK(T) Bandar Mentakab).[[316]](#cite_note-323)

Secondary education (i.e.:[SMK Sura](/wiki/SMK_Sura "SMK Sura"), [Kajang High School](/wiki/Kajang_High_School "Kajang High School")) is conducted for five years. In the final year of secondary education, students sit for the [Malaysian Certificate of Education examination](/wiki/Sijil_Pelajaran_Malaysia "Sijil Pelajaran Malaysia").[[317]](#cite_note-324) Since the introduction of the [matriculation programme](/wiki/Malaysian_Matriculation_Programme "Malaysian Matriculation Programme") in 1999, students who completed the 12-month programme in matriculation colleges can enroll in local universities. However, in the matriculation system, only 10% of places are open to non-*bumiputera*[*[jargon](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style#Technical_language "Wikipedia:Manual of Style")*] students.[[318]](#cite_note-325)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Malaysia](/wiki/Culture_of_Malaysia "Culture of Malaysia")

[![The wooden frame of a house under construction, with the floor raised off the ground](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Salarom_Sabah_Frame-of-a-new-house-01.jpg/250px-Salarom_Sabah_Frame-of-a-new-house-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Salarom_Sabah_Frame-of-a-new-house-01.jpg)

A traditional house being built in [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah")

Malaysia has a multi-ethnic, multicultural, and multilingual society. Substantial influence exists from [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture") and [Indian cultures](/wiki/Culture_of_India "Culture of India"), dating back to when foreign trade began. Other cultural influences include the [Persian](/wiki/Culture_of_Iran "Culture of Iran"), [Arabic](/wiki/Arab_culture "Arab culture"), and [British cultures](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom"). Due to the structure of the government, coupled with the [social contract](/wiki/Social_contract_(Malaysia) "Social contract (Malaysia)") theory, there has been minimal [cultural assimilation](/wiki/Cultural_assimilation "Cultural assimilation") of ethnic minorities.[[319]](#cite_note-326) Some cultural disputes exist between Malaysia and neighbouring countries, notably [Indonesia](/wiki/Culture_of_Indonesia "Culture of Indonesia").[[320]](#cite_note-327)

In 1971, the government created a "National Cultural Policy", defining Malaysian culture. It stated that Malaysian culture must be based on the culture of the indigenous peoples of Malaysia, that it may incorporate suitable elements from other cultures, and that Islam must play a part in it.[[321]](#cite_note-Papers-328) It also promoted the [Malay language](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language") above others.[[322]](#cite_note-Cinema-329) This government intervention into culture has caused resentment among non-Malays who feel their cultural freedom was lessened. Both Chinese and Indian associations have submitted memorandums to the government, accusing it of formulating an undemocratic culture policy.[[321]](#cite_note-Papers-328)

### Fine arts

Main article: [Malaysian art](/wiki/Malaysian_art "Malaysian art")

See also: [Music of Malaysia](/wiki/Music_of_Malaysia "Music of Malaysia") and [Malaysian literature](/wiki/Malaysian_literature "Malaysian literature")

[![Making Malaysian batik](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Malaysian_Batik.jpg/250px-Malaysian_Batik.jpg)](/wiki/File:Malaysian_Batik.jpg)

A craftsman making [batik](/wiki/Batik "Batik"). [Malaysian batik](/wiki/Malaysian_batik "Malaysian batik") is usually patterned with floral motifs with light colouring.

Traditional Malaysian art was mainly centred on the areas of [carving](/wiki/Carving "Carving"), [weaving](/wiki/Weaving "Weaving"), and [silversmithing](/wiki/Silversmith "Silversmith").[[323]](#cite_note-330) Traditional art ranges from handwoven [baskets](/wiki/Basket "Basket") from rural areas to the [silverwork](/wiki/Household_silver "Household silver") of the Malay courts. Common artworks included ornamental [kris](/wiki/Kris "Kris"), [beetle nut](/wiki/Beetle_nut "Beetle nut") sets, and woven [batik](/wiki/Batik "Batik") and [songket](/wiki/Songket "Songket") fabrics. Indigenous East Malaysians are known for their wooden [masks](/wiki/Mask "Mask").[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) Each ethnic group have distinct performing arts, with little overlap between them. However, Malay art does show some [North Indian](/wiki/North_India "North India") influence due to the historical influence of [India](/wiki/Indian_art "Indian art").[[324]](#cite_note-Garland-331)

Traditional Malay music and performing arts appear to have originated in the [Kelantan](/wiki/Kelantan "Kelantan")-[Pattani](/wiki/Patani_(historical_region) "Patani (historical region)") region with influences from [India](/wiki/Music_of_India "Music of India"), [China](/wiki/Music_of_China "Music of China"), [Thailand](/wiki/Music_of_Thailand "Music of Thailand"), and [Indonesia](/wiki/Music_of_Indonesia "Music of Indonesia"). The music is based around [percussion instruments](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument"),[[324]](#cite_note-Garland-331) the most important of which is the [gendang](/wiki/Gendang "Gendang") (drum). There are at least 14 types of traditional drums.[[325]](#cite_note-q694-332) [Drums](/wiki/Drum "Drum") and other traditional percussion instruments and are often made from natural materials.[[325]](#cite_note-q694-332) [Music](/wiki/Music "Music") is traditionally used for storytelling, celebrating life-cycle events, and occasions such as a harvest.[[324]](#cite_note-Garland-331) It was once used as a form of long-distance communication.[[325]](#cite_note-q694-332) In [East Malaysia](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia"), [gong](/wiki/Gong "Gong")-based musical ensembles such as [agung](/wiki/Agung "Agung") and [kulintang](/wiki/Kulintang "Kulintang") are commonly used in ceremonies such as [funerals](/wiki/Funeral "Funeral") and [weddings](/wiki/Wedding "Wedding").[[326]](#cite_note-Matusky-333) These ensembles are also common in neighbouring regions such as in [Mindanao](/wiki/Mindanao "Mindanao") in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Kalimantan](/wiki/Kalimantan "Kalimantan") in [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia"), and [Brunei](/wiki/Brunei "Brunei").[[326]](#cite_note-Matusky-333)

Malaysia has a strong oral tradition that has existed since before the arrival of writing and continues today. Each of the Malay Sultanates created their own literary tradition, influenced by pre-existing oral stories and by the stories that came with Islam.[[327]](#cite_note-EncyclopediaMy-334) The first Malay literature was in the [Arabic script](/wiki/Arabic_script "Arabic script"). The earliest known Malay writing is on the [Terengganu Stone](/wiki/Terengganu_Inscription_Stone "Terengganu Inscription Stone"), made in 1303.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) Chinese and Indian literature became common as the number of speakers increased in Malaysia, and locally produced works based on languages from those areas began to be produced in the 19th century.[[327]](#cite_note-EncyclopediaMy-334) English has also become a common literary language.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) In 1971, the government took the step of defining the literature of different languages. Literature written in Malay was called "the national literature of Malaysia", literature in other *bumiputera* languages was called "regional literature", while literature in other languages was called "sectional literature".[[322]](#cite_note-Cinema-329) Malay poetry is highly developed, and uses many forms. The *Hikayat* form is popular, and the *pantun* has spread from Malay to other languages.[[327]](#cite_note-EncyclopediaMy-334)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Malaysian cuisine](/wiki/Malaysian_cuisine "Malaysian cuisine")

[![A glass of Teh tarik](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Teh_Tarik.jpg/120px-Teh_Tarik.jpg)](/wiki/File:Teh_Tarik.jpg)

*[Teh tarik](/wiki/Teh_tarik "Teh tarik")*

[![Nasi lemak in a plate](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Nasi_lemak.jpg/250px-Nasi_lemak.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nasi_lemak.jpg)

*[Nasi lemak](/wiki/Nasi_lemak "Nasi lemak")*

The national drink and dish of Malaysia[[328]](#cite_note-335)[[329]](#cite_note-336)

Malaysia's cuisine reflects the multi-ethnic makeup of its population.[[330]](#cite_note-Eckhardt-337) Many cultures from within the country and from surrounding regions have greatly influenced the cuisine. Much of the influence comes from the Malay, [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_culture "Chinese culture"), [Indian](/wiki/Culture_of_India "Culture of India"), [Thai](/wiki/Culture_of_Thailand "Culture of Thailand"), [Javanese](/wiki/Javanese_culture "Javanese culture"), and Sumatran cultures,[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) largely due to the country being part of the ancient [spice route](/wiki/Spice_route "Spice route").[[331]](#cite_note-Independent-338) The cuisine is similar to those of [Singapore](/wiki/Singaporean_cuisine "Singaporean cuisine") and Brunei,[[208]](#cite_note-Richmond2-215) and also bears resemblance to [Filipino cuisine](/wiki/Filipino_cuisine "Filipino cuisine").[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) The different states have varied dishes,[[208]](#cite_note-Richmond2-215) and often the food in Malaysia is different from the original dishes.[[263]](#cite_note-Barbara-270)

Sometimes food not found in its original culture is assimilated into another; for example, [Chinese restaurants](/wiki/Chinese_restaurant "Chinese restaurant") in Malaysia often serve Malay dishes.[[332]](#cite_note-339) Food from one culture is sometimes also cooked using styles taken from another culture,[[208]](#cite_note-Richmond2-215) For example, *[sambal](/wiki/Sambal "Sambal") belacan* ([shrimp paste](/wiki/Shrimp_paste "Shrimp paste")) are commonly used as ingredients by Chinese restaurants to create the [stir fried water spinach](/wiki/Stir_fried_water_spinach "Stir fried water spinach") (*kangkung belacan*).[[333]](#cite_note-340) This means that although much of Malaysian food can be traced back to a certain culture, they have their own identity.[[331]](#cite_note-Independent-338) [Rice](/wiki/Rice "Rice") is a staple food and an important constituent of the country's culture.[[334]](#cite_note-341) [Chili](/wiki/Chili_pepper "Chili pepper") is commonly found in local cuisine, although this does not necessarily make them spicy.[[330]](#cite_note-Eckhardt-337)

### Media

Main article: [Mass media in Malaysia](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Malaysia "Mass media in Malaysia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/b6/RTM_Logo_2021.svg/250px-RTM_Logo_2021.svg.png)](/wiki/File:RTM_Logo_2021.svg)

Logo of [Radio Televisyen Malaysia](/wiki/Radio_Televisyen_Malaysia "Radio Televisyen Malaysia"), the country's main public broadcaster

Malaysia's main newspapers are owned by the government and political parties in the ruling coalition,[[335]](#cite_note-342)[[336]](#cite_note-BBC-343) although some major opposition parties also have their own, which are openly sold alongside regular newspapers. A divide exists between the media in the two halves of the country. [Peninsular](/wiki/Peninsular_Malaysia "Peninsular Malaysia")-based media gives a low priority to news from [the East](/wiki/East_Malaysia "East Malaysia") and often treats the eastern states as colonies of the peninsula.[[337]](#cite_note-Mirror-344) As a result of this, [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") launched [TV Sarawak](/wiki/TV_Sarawak "TV Sarawak") as an internet streaming beginning in 2014, and later as a TV station on 10 October 2020[[338]](#cite_note-345) to overcome the low priority and coverage of Peninsular-based media and to solidify the representation of East Malaysia.[[339]](#cite_note-346) The media have been blamed for increasing tension between [Indonesia](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Indonesia "Mass media in Indonesia") and Malaysia, and giving [Malaysians](/wiki/Malaysians "Malaysians") a bad image of [Indonesians](/wiki/Indonesians "Indonesians").[[340]](#cite_note-347) The country has Malay, English, Chinese, and Tamil daily newspapers.[[337]](#cite_note-Mirror-344) Kadazandusun and Bajau news are available only via TV broadcast [Berita RTM](/wiki/Berita_RTM "Berita RTM").[[341]](#cite_note-Berita_RTM-348) Written Kadazan news was once included in publications such as [The Borneo Post](/wiki/The_Borneo_Post "The Borneo Post"), the Borneo Mail, the [Daily Express](/wiki/Daily_Express_(Malaysia) "Daily Express (Malaysia)"), and the [New Sabah Times](/wiki/New_Sabah_Times "New Sabah Times"), but publication has ceased with the newspaper or as a section.[[342]](#cite_note-349)[[343]](#cite_note-350)

[Freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press") is limited, with numerous restrictions on publishing rights and information dissemination.[[344]](#cite_note-351) The government has previously tried to crack down on opposition papers before elections.[[336]](#cite_note-BBC-343) In 2007, a government agency issued a directive to all private television and radio stations to refrain from broadcasting speeches made by opposition leaders,[[345]](#cite_note-352) a move condemned by politicians from the opposition [Democratic Action Party](/wiki/Democratic_Action_Party "Democratic Action Party").[[346]](#cite_note-353) Sabah, where all tabloids but one are independent of government control, has the freest press in Malaysia.[[337]](#cite_note-Mirror-344) Laws such as the [Printing Presses and Publications Act](/wiki/Printing_Presses_and_Publications_Act "Printing Presses and Publications Act") have also been cited as curtailing freedom of expression.[[347]](#cite_note-McAdams-354)

### Holidays and festivals

Main article: [Public holidays in Malaysia](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Malaysia "Public holidays in Malaysia")

[![Temple at night illuminated with light from decorations](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Kek_Lok_Si_during_Chinese_New_Year_2025.jpg/250px-Kek_Lok_Si_during_Chinese_New_Year_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kek_Lok_Si_during_Chinese_New_Year_2025.jpg)

Malaysia's largest [Buddhist temple](/wiki/Buddhist_temple "Buddhist temple")—[Kek Lok Si](/wiki/Kek_Lok_Si "Kek Lok Si") in [Penang](/wiki/Penang "Penang")—illuminated in preparation for [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year")

Malaysians observe several holidays and festivities throughout the year. Some are federally gazetted [public holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays "Public holidays") and some are observed by individual states. Other festivals are observed by particular ethnic or religious groups, and the main holiday of each major group has been declared a public holiday. Malaysia has two official national holidays: *[Hari Merdeka](/wiki/Hari_Merdeka "Hari Merdeka")* (Independence Day) on 31 August, commemorating the independence of the Federation of Malaya in 1957--is the most widely observed holiday[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189)--and the more recent [Malaysia Day](/wiki/Malaysia_Day "Malaysia Day") on 16 September (since 2010) which commemorates the Malaysia Federation in 1963 with the states of [Sabah](/wiki/Sabah "Sabah") and [Sarawak](/wiki/Sarawak "Sarawak") both in East Malaysia.[[348]](#cite_note-355) Other notable national holidays are [Labour Day](/wiki/Labour_Day "Labour Day") (1 May) and the King's birthday (first week of June).[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189)

[Muslim holidays](/wiki/Muslim_holidays "Muslim holidays") are prominent as Islam is the state religion; *Hari Raya Puasa* (also called *Hari Raya Aidilfitri*, Malay for [Eid al-Fitr](/wiki/Eid_al-Fitr "Eid al-Fitr")), *Hari Raya Haji* (also called *Hari Raya Aidiladha*, Malay for [Eid al-Adha](/wiki/Eid_al-Adha "Eid al-Adha")), *[Maulidur Rasul](/wiki/Maulidur_Rasul "Maulidur Rasul")* (birthday of the Prophet), and others being observed.[[182]](#cite_note-Marshall-189) Malaysian Chinese celebrate festivals such as [Chinese New Year](/wiki/Chinese_New_Year "Chinese New Year") and others relating to traditional Chinese beliefs. [Wesak](/wiki/Wesak "Wesak") Day is observed and celebrated by Buddhists. Hindus in Malaysia celebrate *[Deepavali](/wiki/Deepavali "Deepavali")*, the festival of lights,[[349]](#cite_note-MSU-356) while *[Thaipusam](/wiki/Thaipusam "Thaipusam")* is a religious rite which sees pilgrims from all over the country converge at the [Batu Caves](/wiki/Batu_Caves "Batu Caves").[[350]](#cite_note-357) Malaysia's Christian community celebrates most of the holidays observed by Christians elsewhere, most notably Christmas and Easter. In addition to this, the Dayak community in Sarawak celebrate a harvest festival known as *[Gawai](/wiki/Gawai_Dayak "Gawai Dayak")*,[[351]](#cite_note-358) and the Kadazandusun community celebrate *[Kaamatan](/wiki/Kaamatan "Kaamatan")*.[[352]](#cite_note-359) Despite most festivals being identified with a particular ethnic or religious group, celebrations are universal. In a custom known as "open house" Malaysians participate in the celebrations of others, often visiting the houses of those who identify with the festival.[[246]](#cite_note-MIDA-253)

### Sports

Main article: [Sport in Malaysia](/wiki/Sport_in_Malaysia "Sport in Malaysia")

[![A woman and a man in black outfits with red belts practising the martial art of Silat Melayu](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Seni_silat_melayu.jpg/250px-Seni_silat_melayu.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seni_silat_melayu.jpg)

Traditional sports, such as the martial art style [Silat Melayu](/wiki/Silat_Melayu "Silat Melayu"), persist alongside modern sports.

Popular sports in Malaysia include [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football"), [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [field hockey](/wiki/Field_hockey "Field hockey"), [bowls](/wiki/Bowls "Bowls"), [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis"), [squash](/wiki/Squash_(sport) "Squash (sport)"), [martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts"), [horse riding](/wiki/Horse_riding "Horse riding"), [sailing](/wiki/Sailing "Sailing"), and [skate boarding](/wiki/Skate_boarding "Skate boarding").[[246]](#cite_note-MIDA-253) Football is the most popular sport in Malaysia.[[353]](#cite_note-360) Badminton matches also attract thousands of spectators, and since 1948 Malaysia has been one of four countries to hold the [Thomas Cup](/wiki/Thomas_Cup "Thomas Cup"), the world team championship trophy of men's badminton.[[354]](#cite_note-361) The Malaysian Lawn Bowls Federation was registered in 1997.[[355]](#cite_note-362) Squash was brought to the country by members of the British army, with the first competition being held in 1939.[[356]](#cite_note-363) The [Squash Racquets Association of Malaysia](/wiki/Squash_Racquets_Association_of_Malaysia "Squash Racquets Association of Malaysia") was created on 25 June 1972.[[357]](#cite_note-364) The men's [national field hockey team](/wiki/Malaysia_men%27s_national_field_hockey_team "Malaysia men's national field hockey team") ranked [10th in the world](/wiki/FIH_Men%27s_World_Ranking "FIH Men's World Ranking") as of June 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Malaysia&action=edit).[[358]](#cite_note-365) The 3rd [Hockey World Cup](/wiki/Hockey_World_Cup "Hockey World Cup") was hosted at [Merdeka Stadium](/wiki/Merdeka_Stadium "Merdeka Stadium") in Kuala Lumpur, as well as the 10th cup.[[359]](#cite_note-366) The country also has its own [Formula One](/wiki/Formula_One "Formula One") track—the [Sepang International Circuit](/wiki/Sepang_International_Circuit "Sepang International Circuit"), with the first [Malaysian Grand Prix](/wiki/Malaysian_Grand_Prix "Malaysian Grand Prix") held in 1999.[[360]](#cite_note-367) Traditional sports include [Silat Melayu](/wiki/Silat_Melayu "Silat Melayu"), the most common style of martial arts practised by [ethnic Malays](/wiki/Ethnic_Malays "Ethnic Malays").[[361]](#cite_note-368)

The Federation of Malaya Olympic Council was formed in 1953, and received recognition by the IOC in 1954. It first participated in the [1956 Melbourne Olympic Games](/wiki/1956_Melbourne_Olympic_Games "1956 Melbourne Olympic Games"). The council was renamed the [Olympic Council of Malaysia](/wiki/Olympic_Council_of_Malaysia "Olympic Council of Malaysia") in 1964, and [has participated](/wiki/Malaysia_at_the_Olympics "Malaysia at the Olympics") in all but one Olympic games since its inception. The largest number of athletes ever sent to the Olympics was 57 to the [1972 Munich Olympic Games](/wiki/1972_Munich_Olympic_Games "1972 Munich Olympic Games").[[362]](#cite_note-369) Besides the Olympic Games, Malaysia also participates in the [Paralympic Games](/wiki/Paralympic_Games "Paralympic Games").[[363]](#cite_note-370) Malaysia has competed at the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games") since 1950 as Malaya, and 1966 as Malaysia, and the games were hosted in Kuala Lumpur in 1998.[[364]](#cite_note-371)[[365]](#cite_note-372)
